translation:
1. Oh, Sleep was walking by the windows,  
And Drowsiness by the fence.  
2. Sleep asks Drowsiness:  
– Where will we spend the night?  
3. Where will we spend the night?  
What will we have for dinner?  
4. – There we will spend the night,  
Where the little child is,  
Where the little house is warm.  
5. There we will spend the night,  
Rocking the little child.  
Eh, eh, eh!

1. Oh, Sleep walks by the windows,  
And Drowsiness by the fence.  
2. Sleep asks Drowsiness:  
– Where will we spend the night?  
3. – Where the little house is warm,  
Where the small child is.  
4. There we will spend the night,  
Rocking the little child.

1. Oh, the Sleep walks by the windows,  
And the Drowsiness by the fence.  
2. Sleep asks Drowsiness:  
– Where shall we spend the night?  
3. – Where the little house is warm,  
Where the little child is small.

1. Oh, the Sleep walks by the windows,  
And the Drowsiness by the fence.  
2. Sleep asks Drowsiness:  
– Where shall we spend the night?  
3. – Where the little house is warm,  
Where the little child is.  
4. There we will spend the night,  
Rocking the little child.  
       *       *       *  
To the same melody:  
1. Oh, sleep, child, I am rocking you,  
When you fall asleep, I will leave you.  
2. I will leave you by the water,  
Cover you with dock leaves.  
3. Oh, lul-lu, I will rock you,  
When you fall asleep, I will leave.  
4. I will leave you, you will sleep,  
And I will go to the field to reap.  
5. And I will go to work,  
And will return only on Saturday.  
6. Oh li, lul-lu, lul-lu,  
Oh ni, lul-lu, lul-lu.

1. [Ah-ah, ah-ah, lullaby,  
To other children, nonsense,]  
2. But to my son, kalachi,  
So that he sleeps day and night.

1. Oh well, lullaby, lullaby,  
The hoots have flown in,  
2. They began to coo...

1. Gray kitty,  
White kitty,  
Don’t walk around the house,  
Don’t wake the baby.  
2. The baby will sleep,  
The kitty will purr.  
And the cat purrs,  
And the baby dozes.  
Ah, ah, ah!

Gray kitty,  
White kitty,  
Don’t walk around the house,  
Don’t wake the baby.

1. Ah, kitty  
Stole a ball of yarn from Sashko  
2. And carried it outside the forest,  
All the way to Petya.  
3. Petya started to hit the cat,  
He began to teach the kitty:  
4. – Don’t learn to steal, cat,  
Learn to work –  
To sew little boots.  
5. Boots made of bulrush  
Are not afraid of water.

1. The cat fell from the gate,
And also injured its belly.
2. The kitty fell from the stove
And bruised its shoulders.

1. Two cats, two cats,  
Both gray and white,  
2. They walked around the pantry,  
Causing harm to people.  
Ah-ah-ah!

1. Luli, luli, little lullabies,  
Silky little chains,  
2. A flaxen cradle,  
And a noble child. Ah, ah, ah!  
3. To rock the cradle,  
To lull the child to sleep. Ah!

1. Ah-ah, ah-ah, a child  
With black eyes,  
2. And the cradle is a lady’s,  
And the child is a noble’s.  
3. Luli, luli, luleshki,  
The little chains broke.  
4. The new chain broke,  
The little child cried.  
5. I do not grieve for the new chain,  
But I grieve for the little child.  
6. Ah-ah, ah-ah, oh daughter.  
Ah-ah, luli, luli, luleshki.

1. Oh well, child, go to sleep,  
Because mother has gone somewhere.  
She went to the valley  
To pick viburnum.  
2. To pick viburnum,  
To give the child drink,  
So that the child is healthy,  
Has dark eyebrows,  
Rosy cheeks,  
Oh, sleep a little more.  
Ah, ah, ah!

Luli, luli,  
Grandfather went to get some onions,  
And grandfather went to get some parsley  
To season Maria’s soup.

1. – How are you?  
Where have you been? – At grandmother’s.  
2. – What did you eat? – Porridge.  
– And what did you drink? – Brew.  
3. The porridge is soft,  
The brew is sweet,  
4. Grandmother is old,  
Lidusya is little.

1. Go, go, little rain,  
I will cook you some borscht.  
2. I will hang on the oak,  
I will call the pigeon.  
3. While the pigeon flies here,  
The borscht will boil.

1. The white hen with a crest  
Was walking around the yard,  
Leading behind her  
Twenty-three chicks.  
2. And when the bad hawk  
Was seen by the hen,  
She called all the chicks  
Close to herself.  
3. The chicks ran together,  
Hid under the hen,  
And quietly laughed  
At the bad hawk.

1. Carol, carol, caroling,  
A good honey flatbread,  
2. But without honey, not the same,  
Give me, uncle, a five-kopeck coin,  
3. And you, auntie, money,  
So that it is good.

1. Carol, carol, caroler,  
A good honeyed flatbread,  
2. But without honey, not the same,  
Give me, uncle, a coin,  
3. And you, aunt, money,  
You will be good.

1. A little heifer ran  
From the birch grove  
Into the uncle's yard.  
Good evening!

1. Shadrik-vedrik,  
Bring a dumpling.  
2. A lump of porridge,  
A ring of sausage.

1. And Jesus Christ was born,  
He took his place on the throne.  
2. And kiss Jesus Christ on the feet,  
And bring us, the children, pastries.

1. A white cloud is swirling outside,  
A caroler is shaking under the window,  
Let him into the house,  
And he will sing  
Carol songs.  

2. We let him into the house,  
He began to sing carols,  
When he saw the pies,  
He was overwhelmed with grief  
Forever.  

3. He ate seven, the eighth one was gone,  
Then he climbed up for the lard,  
But he didn’t get the lard,  
He fell down from the ladder,  
He got hurt.  

4. And he walks through the hallway, crying,  
Because he won’t see the door at night,  
Yosyp opened the door,  
Caught him by the hair  
And pulls him.  

5. – Yosyp, don’t beat me,  
I won’t walk around at night,  
Because from that carol  
Trouble comes,  
Forever.

1. The Sleep walks by the windows,  
And the Drowsiness by the fence.  
2. Sleep asks Drowsiness:  
– Where will we spend the night?  
3. – Where the little warm house is,  
Where the little child is.  
4. There we will spend the night,  
Rocking the little child.

1. Oh, Sleep walks by the windows,  
And Slumber by the fence.  
2. Sleep asks Slumber:  
– Where shall we spend the night?  
3. – Where the little warm house is,  
Where the little child is.  
4. There we will spend the night,  
Rocking the little child.  
5. Oh sleep, child, I rock you,  
When you fall asleep, I will leave you.

1. Lullaby, lullaby,
Give me a clapper.
2. Oh lullaby, lullaby,
I give a clapper.

1. Luli, luli, lulyechky,  
Painted garlands,  
2. And the lady's cradle,  
The noble child sleeps.

1. Cat, cat, little cat  
Stole a ball of yarn from Slavko.  
2. Ran, ran under the forest,  
All the way to Valya carried it.  
3. Valya scolded the kitty:  
– Don’t learn, kitty, to steal,  
4. But learn to work,  
To sew little shoes,  
5. To carry them to the city,  
And sell them in the city,  
6. To earn money,  
To buy candies.

1. The kitty went to fetch some water,  
I-nya-nya.  
2. The kitty went to rescue,  
I-nya-nya.  
3. Nowhere to be found is the kitty,  
I-nya-nya.  
4. The kitty took...

1. Oh sleep, child, I will rock you,  
And when you fall asleep, I will leave you.  
2. I will go to work myself,  
And I won’t come back until Saturday.

1. Oh sleep, child, I lull you,  
And when you fall asleep, I will leave you.  
2. I will put you under the bench,  
I myself will go outside.  
3. And on the street a violin plays,  
My mother does not let me go.  
4. – Let me go, mother, let me go, mother,  
To walk outside.

1. Oh sleep, child, I will rock you,  
But if you don't sleep, I will leave you.  
2. I will throw you under the bench,  
I myself will go out to the street.  
3. Let the mice squeak at you,  
Let the boys love me.  
4. The pipe plays on the street,  
My mother won't let me go.  
5. – Let me go, mother, for an hour,  
I will rock the child.

1. Lulyu, luli, lulyu,  
The woman went to get some onions,  
2. And the old man went for parsley  
To season Yulia’s soup.  
3. But the onions did not grow,  
The woman got lost in the thicket.

(DS-KZ-08+DS-KD-01+DS-KZ-02)  
1. Lulyu, lulyu, old lullaby,  
Don’t go to the garden for onions.  
2. Because the onion is sleepy,  
And I am a little child.  

*       *       *  

1. Sleep walks near the windows,  
And Drowsiness walks near the fence.  
2. Sleep asks Drowsiness:  
– Where shall we spend the night?  
3. – Where the little warm hut is,  
Where the little child is.  
4. Oh, you will rock the cradle,  
And I will lull to sleep.  

*       *       *  

1. So sleep, child, I will rock you,  
Because if you fall asleep, I will leave you.  
2. I will lay you under the bench,  
Cover you with a chintz.

1. Lulye, lulye, lullabies,  
Mother went to her daughter,  
2. And from the daughter to the son,  
There was a bitter visit.

1. Tosі, tosi,  
Pigs in the peas,  
Calves in the cabbage;  
2. I wish they  
Would perish,  
How fat they are.

1. Caw, caw, crow  
Was cooking porridge for the children,  
Cooling it on the doorstep:  
2. – Run, children, get some firewood,  
The porridge will be ready;  
3. Run, children, get some sticks,  
I'll give you some porridge in bits.  
4. I'll give this one, I'll give that one,  
But this one I'll twist the head, twist, twist!  
5. Hey, hey, they flew,  
And sat on the head!

Sit next to me,  
Stretch out your little legs,  
And I will kiss you,  
And you will hug me.

1. A gentleman is riding, riding,  
On his little horse, alone, alone.  
2. |: Riding, riding, hop, hop,  
On his little horse, hop, hop. :|  
3. A Jew is riding, riding,  
On his little horse, hitz, hitz, hitz, hitz.  
4. And behind the Jew, Jewish girls  
Went around near the fence.

1. And the sheep asked the lamb:  
– Mow some hay for me, little lamb.  
2. But God knows what kind of winter it will be,  
Whether I will come out alive in the spring.

1. – What are you doing, grandpa?  
– I am making a basket.  
2. – Why do you need the basket?  
– To collect ashes.  
3. – Why do you need the ashes?  
– To light the firewood.  
4. – Where is that firewood?  
– In the brick house.  
5. – Where is that house?  
– Overgrown with periwinkle.  
6. – Where is that periwinkle?  
– It saved the calf.  
7. – Where is that calf?  
– The pestle killed it.  
8. – Where is that pestle?  
– The tombstones wore it down.  
9. – Where are those tombstones?  
– The beetles ate them.  
10. – Where are those beetles?  
– At God's doorstep.  
Cock-a-doodle-doo!

1. The carpenters are great  
They make tables, chairs  
2. With a hammer on the chisel,  
Golden shavings fly.  
3. With saws, tick, tick,  
With hammers, knock, knock,  
We have golden hands for work.

1. Boyar, on that side,  
Boyar, at the gathering,  
The gypsies were standing,  
Yes, the gypsies were standing.  
2. Boyar, among them,  
Boyar, among the gypsies,  
A gypsy fortune-teller,  
Yes, a gypsy fortune-teller.  
3. Boyar, to that gypsy girl,  
Boyar, to the fortune-teller,  
There is a well-trodden path,  
[Yes, a well-trodden path.]  
4. Boyar, along that path,  
Boyar, along the road,  
A girl was walking,  
[Yes, a girl was walking.]  
5. Boyar, she, that  
Boyar, gypsy girl,  
Faithfully asked,  
[Yes, faithfully asked:]  
6. – Boyar, gypsy girl,  
Boyar, fortune-teller,  
Grant my wish,  
[Yes, grant my wish:]  
7. Boyar, enchant, enchant,  
Boyar, little Cossack,  
To be with me forever,  
[Yes, to be with me forever.]  
8. Boyar, on that side,  
Boyar, at the gathering,  
The magical herb swirls,  
[Yes, the magical herb swirls,]  
9. Boyar, above her, yes  
Boyar, the girl,  
Three Cossacks are fighting,  
[Yes, three Cossacks are fighting.]  
10. Boyar, one Cossack,  
Boyar, by the table,  
Writes tiny letters,  
[Yes, writes tiny letters,]  
11. Boyar, the second Cossack,  
Boyar, to the girl,  
Sprinkles coins,  
[Yes, sprinkles coins,]  
12. Boyar, the third Cossack,  
Boyar, by the threshold,  
Sheds tiny tears,  
[Yes, sheds tiny tears.]

1. Oh rise, rise,  
Also the bright moon,  
Also the bright star.  
2. Also the bright star...  
Oh come, come,  
Bold young man,  
To visit me.  
3. To visit me...  
Oh drink, drink,  
Bold young man,  
At least a glass of wine.  
4. At least a glass of wine...  
– Oh drink, drink,  
Bold girl,  
At the forefront herself.  
5. At the forefront herself...  
Oh if only you,  
Young man,  
Had not cast spells on me.  
6. Had not cast spells on me...  
Oh if only you,  
Young man,  
Had not enchanted me.  
7. Oh early on Sunday,  
Very early in the morning,  
All the bells are ringing.  
8. All the bells are ringing...  
Oh now that,  
Young man,  
Is being carried to the grave.  
9. Is being carried to the grave...  
Oh behind him, behind him  
His own mother  
Is grieving.  
10. And the girl stands  
At the new gate,  
Smiling:  
11. – Oh if only you,  
Young Cossack,  
Did not love the three of us.  
12. And now you will,  
Young Cossack,  
Rot in the damp earth.

1. Oh rise, rise,  
Also the bright moon,  
Also the bright star.  
2. Also the bright star...  
Oh come, come,  
Bold young man,  
To visit me.  
3. To visit me...  
Oh drink, drink,  
Bold young man,  
At least a glass of wine.  
4. At least a glass of wine...  
– Oh drink, drink,  
Bold girl,  
Sitting in front.  
5. Sitting in front...  
Oh may you not,  
Young man,  
Cast spells on me.  
6. Cast spells on me...  
Oh may you not,  
Young man,  
Bewitch me.  
7. Oh early Sunday morning,  
Very early in the morning,  
All the bells are ringing.  
8. All the bells are ringing...  
Oh now that young man  
Is being carried to the grave.  
9. Being carried to the grave...  
Oh behind him, behind him,  
His own mother  
Is grieving.  
10. And the girl stands  
In new gates,  
Smiling:  
11. – Oh if only you,  
Young Cossack,  
Would not love the three of us.  
12. And now you will,  
Young Cossack,  
Rot in the damp earth.

Filename: Kmeln_ballads_445_Oi de zh zhe ty nochuvav
translation:
1. – Oh, where did you spend the night,  
Son Vasylenko?  
– Oh, at the widow’s on pillows,  
Dear mother’s heart.*

2. – Oh, what did you have for dinner,  
Son Vasylenko?  
– Fish with stewed apples,  
Dear mother’s heart.

3. – Oh, where did she catch it,  
Son Vasylenko?  
– Under the willow tree, in a pile,  
Dear mother’s heart.

4. – With what did she clean it,  
Son Vasylenko?  
– With an axe by the threshold,  
Dear mother’s heart.

5. – In what did she cook it,  
Son Vasylenko?  
– In a little pot by the fire,  
Dear mother’s heart.

6. – Oh, did she eat it herself,  
Son Vasylenko?  
– Oh, she did not eat, she was weak,  
Dear mother’s heart.

7. – And did her children eat it,  
Son Vasylenko?  
– Oh, they did not eat, they wanted to sleep,  
Dear mother’s heart.

8. – And did her servants eat it,  
Son Vasylenko?  
– Oh, they did not eat because they did not want to,  
Dear mother’s heart.

9. – What kind of father do you need,  
Son Vasylenko?  
– Oh, a bearded man with a book,  
Dear mother’s heart.

10. – What kind of friends do you need,  
Son Vasylenko?  
– Two boys with horns,  
Dear mother’s heart.

11. – What kind of matchmakers do you need,  
Son Vasylenko?  
– Three women with candles,  
Dear mother’s heart.

12. – What kind of house do you need,  
Son Vasylenko?  
– Four boards and a little earth,  
Dear mother’s heart.

1. Oh, whose field is that,  
So wide and long?  
Could it be that young Cossack,  
With the dark eyebrows?  

2. Oh, whose oxen are those  
Wandering through the field?  
Could it be that young Cossack,  
Who loves the three of us?  

3. The first one fell in love,  
Bought a little horse,  
The second fell in love,  
Bought a saddle.  

4. The second fell in love,  
Bought a saddle,  
And the third dark-haired girl  
Brought supper:  

5. – Supper is ready, Cossack,  
Do not be sad,  
If you have no wife at home,  
Love me.  

6. – Oh, I do have a wife  
And two little children,  
My heart aches deeply  
For both of them.

1. Oh, whose oxen are those  
Walking by the grove?  
Could it be the young Cossack  
Who loved the three of us?  
  
2. The first loved him,  
The second adored him,  
And the third, the dark-haired one,  
Was preparing a spell.  
  
3. She prepared a spell –  
A gray quail –  
And placed it before the Cossack  
On a white plate.  
  
4. The Cossack sat down, having eaten  
The wings and the ribs, –  
His head began to ache,  
Burning near his heart.  
  
5. When his mother heard  
From the third room:  
– Oh, my son, where will you  
Find doctors?  
  
6. – They will not help, mother,  
Neither medicine nor doctors,  
Only a fathom of earth  
And four boards will help.

1. Oh, rye on the hill, (and)  
Rye in the valley,  
Under the white birch  
Lies a slain Cossack.  

2. Oh, killed and laid to rest,  
And wrapped in rye,  
Covered with black silk,  
His face covered with a Chinese cloth.  

3. Oh, a girl came  
And began to weep,  
Oh, another came,  
But she was not the same.  

4. Oh, another came,  
But she was not the same,  
She lifted the Chinese cloth  
And kissed him.  

5. – Oh, rise, Cossack,  
Rise, young one,  
Your black horse  
Wanders through the grove.  

6. – Oh, let him wander,  
Oh, let him roam,  
|: The hour will come  
When he will wake me. :|

1. Oh, in the city of Jerusalem  
There was news:  
Hannusya bewitched  
A widow's son.  

2. But how could she have bewitched him –  
She called him into the house,  
Told me, the young man,  
To sit on the bench.  

3. Oh, I barely, young as I was,  
Managed to sit on the bench,  
When Hannusya gave me  
Three apples to eat.  

4. Oh, when I ate the three apples, –  
My heart began to ache,  
And when my mother found out, –  
She fainted from sorrow.  

5. – Give me, comrades,  
A down pillow,  
Pray, pray, and bring to me  
My dear Hannusya.  

6. Oh, when Hannusya came,  
She stood on the doorstep,  
Small tears  
Were streaming down Hannusya.  

7. – Don’t cry, don’t cry, Hannusya,  
There’s nothing to cry for,  
My son was bewitched  
By your own mother as well.  

8. – It wasn’t me who bewitched him,  
Nor was it my mother –  
It was the neighbor woman  
From the third house.  

9. – I have a little cherry tree –  
I can’t reach the top:  
Oh, who will love you,  
If I stop loving?  

10. – I have a little cherry tree  
Greening at the top.  
When you grow up – a second will come,  
I am still young.

1. Ivan walks over the Danube,  
Playing a pipe;  
Paraska draws water,  
Winks at Ivan:  
2. – Come, Ivan, dear Ivan,  
Come, my heart,  
Let's drink some vodka  
Good, with pepper.  
3. Ivan came to Paraska  
And stood on the doorstep,  
She gave him a glass to eat  
In a wheat pie.  
4. In one corner of the pie  
Is green rue,  
In the other corner of the pie  
Is a fierce serpent.  
5. Ivan came home,  
Bowed his head,  
His mother asks him:  
– Where have you been, son?  
6. – At Paraska's, my mother,  
In Paraska's house,  
She bewitched me, mother,  
Saying that I must die.  
7. His mother told him:  
– Go, son, plow.  
– Oh, I won't go, my mother,  
Because I must die.  
8. His mother told him:  
– Go, son, mow.  
– Oh, I won't go, my mother,  
Because I won’t live.  
9. Call, my mother,  
The young lads,  
Call, my mother,  
The young lads.  
10. The lads and young men came,  
Lifted their hats:  
– Oh, what do you need,  
Widower Ivan?  
11. – Oh, sit down, brothers,  
I’ll tell you –  
Which girl truly loves,  
Don’t go to her house.  
12. Oh, give me, my mother,  
A white pillow,  
Oh, carry me, brothers,  
Under the green pear tree.  
13. Call, my mother,  
Paraska dear.  
Paraska came,  
Stood on the doorstep.  
14. – Why don’t you come, Paraska,  
Closer, unkind one?  
See now, Paraska,  
You have bewitched my son?  
15. – I didn’t bewitch him,  
Nor is it my mother,  
It was the neighbor,  
From the third house.

1. A black cloud is coming,  
A blue one is coming,  
And the rich widow's daughter  
Has enchanted the son.  

2. And as she was to enchant him,  
She called him to the house:  
"Come, come, little Cossack,  
I have something to tell you."  

3. And when he entered the house,  
He collapsed on the stack,  
They gave him a glass of rum,  
So the Cossack would drink.  

4. "Drink, drink, little Cossack,  
This little glass of rum,  
And hurry up, little Cossack,  
Hurry home quickly."  

5. And when he came home,  
He sat on the bench,  
His mother asks him:  
"Where have you been, my son?"  

6. "I was, my dear mother,  
At the rich daughter’s place,  
Prepare, prepare for me, mother,  
A shirt for tomorrow."  

7. "Are you telling the truth, my son,  
Or are you joking like that,  
That you, my dear son,  
Need a shirt?"  

8. "I’m not joking, mother,  
I’m only telling the truth,  
Because tomorrow very early  
I will lie on the bench."  

9. Oh, the Cossack died, oh, the Cossack died,  
Lying on the bench,  
The rich daughter came,  
And stood at the doorstep.  

10. The rich daughter ordered  
To cut down a maple tree,  
To build a coffin  
For the young Cossack.  

11. They hew the maple, they hew the maple,  
They hew the maple wood,  
For the young Cossack  
They make a coffin.  

12. They hew the maple, they hew the maple,  
The splinters fly upward,  
For the young Cossack  
All the girls are crying.  

13. The girls cry, the girls cry,  
The young women cry...  
Whichever one you look at, –  
They are all enchantresses.

1. Oh, I will take  
My entourage by the tails  
And I will go  
To the girl for a little while.  
2. Oh, I came  
To the new gates,  
I am drawn  
To the porch.  
3. [Oh, I approach  
The new porch,]  
Almost got  
Bitten by the dogs.  
4. Oh, I entered  
From the porch into the house:  
– Why didn’t you come out  
To chase the dogs away?  
5. – If I were to be bitten, –  
Let them bite me, –  
I have better  
Benches taken.  
6. Move aside,  
Curly-haired ones, on the bench,  
Let the old lovers  
Sit down.  
7. – Then, girlie, you loved me,  
When you took  
Gifts from me.  
8. – Give me, sister,  
The golden keys,  
Let me throw  
Those gifts to him.  
9. Give me, sister,  
The golden keys,  
Let me throw  
The gifts into his eyes.

1. – Oh, open the gate, Hanusya,  
I am eager to come to you.  
2. Oh, open the stable, Hanusya,  
Let me put my horse inside.  
3. – My father did not build the stable  
For you to put your horse inside,  
4. – Oh, open the hayloft, Hanusya.  
– I already have a better place than you there.  
5. – Oh, open the door, Hanusya,  
Let me see if it's better than me.  
6. I barely got through the threshold,  
They offer me a little chair to sit on.  
7. I barely sat on the little chair,  
They offer me a quail to eat.  
8. I did not finish eating  
Half a wing and some ribs,  
A heavy pain gripped me near the heart.  
9. Oh, like a Cossack,  
I drank water,  
Fell back into the hayloft.  
10. Fell back into the hayloft,  
And the girl laughs at the Cossack through the window.  
11. – Oh, don’t laugh, young girl, at me,  
I’ve already drunk your little glasses of spirits enough.

1. Oh, I was plowing  
In the guelder-rose meadow  
And I broke  
A share of the plow.  
2. I do not know myself,  
What I should do,  
I do not know myself,  
What I should do.  
3. Whether to make a plowshare for the plow,  
Or to go to the girl  
For the whole night?  
4. Oh, I will change  
My clothes to rags  
And I will go  
To the girl a little.  
5. I came  
To her yard,  
The dogs almost  
Did not eat me.  
6. She came out  
To defend from the dogs  
And began  
To reproach me even more:  
7. – Buzz, buzz,  
Evil dogs,  
Drive away  
The unfaithful one from the house.  
8. Buzz, buzz,  
May they eat you,  
Because I have  
The elders sitting here.  
9. – Oh, open up,  
Girl, the door,  
Let me see  
If there is someone better than me.

1. |: I was gathering 
In the guelder-rose meadow, :|
2. |: The stick 
Broke in the meadow. :|
3. (and) I don’t even know 
What I should do, 
I don’t even know 
What I should do. 
4. Whether to look for sticks, 
Sticks to find, 
Or to take the plow on my shoulder, 
And go to the girl? 
5. |: I was driven 
By desire to her. :|
6. I arrived 
By the white walls, 
I was almost 
Eaten by dogs. 
7. The dear one came out, 
As if to shoo away, 
She started 
Even worse to harass: 
8. |: – Buzz, buzz, 
May they bite you. :|
9. I entered 
From the yard to the house, 
There, I have 
No place to set my foot. 
10. – Spread out, 
Curls, along the bench, 
Let him sit 
That unloved one on the bench. 
11. – Since you used to love 
To take gifts, 
Now love 
To receive the guest. 
12. – Oh, I have 
A pit beneath the threshold, 
There I laid 
Your gifts. 
13. |: Give me, mother, 
The golden keys, :|
14. |: Let me throw 
The gifts into the eyes. :|

1. Oh, I burned  
In the guelder-rose meadow*  
2. And I broke  
A shelf in the plow.  
3. Oh, I went  
To carve the shelf,  
4. I came  
Before the white doors,  
5. Almost got  
Eaten by dogs.  
6. The young ones call me  
Into the house,  
7. They tell me  
To sit on the bench.  
8. – Make room,  
Curly-haired ones, on the bench,  
9. Let me sit  
In place of the lover.  
10. They gave me  
A quail to eat.  
11. Oh, I devoured  
The wing and the rib.  
12. – Farewell now,  
My heart.  

*Note: "Калиновий луг" is a traditional poetic image referring to a meadow with guelder-rose (kalyna), a symbol often used in Ukrainian folklore.

1. Oh, Serbin walks on the mountain,
Cutting barley with an axe.  
2. Cutting barley with an axe,
A girl carries food to him.  
3. – Oh Serbine-Serbinochku,
Matchmake me, girl.  
4. – I will not matchmake you,
Because you don’t know how to enchant.  
5. Enchant your dear brother –
You will have a beloved.  
6. – Oh, if I knew magic,
I would enchant him.  
7. – Oh, in the meadow, by the guelder-rose,
A snake hangs on the guelder-rose.  
8. The sun burns on it,
And sap flows from it.  
9. Hold out, dear, a jug, –
So that the sap flows in.  
10. The foolish girl listened,
And poured from the jug ...  
11. Her brother buys a gift in the city,
She prepares magic for him.  
12. Brother comes to her with palms –
She comes to brother with spells.  
13. – Oh brother, here is some beer,
I brewed it myself.  
14. I brewed it myself
And I drank it myself.  
15. Brother listened and got drunk
And just fell on the horse.  
16. – What is this, sister, for beer –
That made my heart wither?  
17. – Oh Serbine, Serbinochku,
Matchmake me, girl.  
18. – I will not matchmake you –
You have learned to enchant.  
19. You enchanted your brother –
You will enchant a young man.  
20. – What have I done, poor me,
That I have neither brother nor Serbin?..

1. A little cuckoo flew over  
And said: – Cuckoo!  
The young women broke  
The loafer's arm.  
2. Oh, how they broke it,  
He told no one,  
He took his little arm under his armpit  
And went home.  
3. Oh, when he came home  
He sat on a bench,  
He leaned his little head  
On the plank table.  
4. – My dear old mother,  
My sweet mother,  
My right arm hurts, –  
So he spoke.  
5. – Let it hurt you,  
Let it not stop hurting,  
Let the other young women  
Not touch you.  
6. Oh, the little cuckoo flew  
Over the oak grove,  
Pecked the wheat,  
Left the chaff.  
7. – Oh, what good is chaff to me,  
When there is no grain?  
Oh, what good are other boys to me,  
When mine is not here?  
8. – Oh, what good is chaff to me,  
When there is no grain?  
Oh, what good are other girls to me,  
When mine are not here?

1. – Oh cherry-cherry tree,  
Why don’t you bear fruit?  
– Young maiden,  
Why don’t you go for a walk?*  

2. – How can I bear fruit,  
When the root is drying up,  
– How can I go for a walk,  
When my beloved leaves me.  

3. A small river flows  
Along the foot of the hill,  
The girl took new buckets,  
And went for water.  

4. She didn’t fill the water,  
She stood and thought,  
When she remembered her beloved,  
She immediately fell into the water.  

5. Father runs, mother runs  
And shouts: – Help!  
But the girl replied:  
– Don’t grieve for me.  

6. I was to be grieved for,  
When I was little,  
But now let the damp earth  
Grieve for me.

1. Around the mill of Kremynyn,  
The viburnum bloomed;  
A young girl learned  
To sleep in the garden.*  

2. She learned to sleep in the garden  
Among the apple trees,  
So she could have a little talk  
All night with the lads.  

3. – Let me go, my mother,  
To walk in the cherry orchard, –  
The first roosters will crow,  
Then I will enter the house.  

4. The roosters crow, others crow too,  
Even the hen clucks.  
The mother calls to have dinner,  
But the daughter does not want to.  

5. – Have dinner, my mother,  
What you have cooked,  
Because for me, my mother,  
Dinner is no longer dear.  

6. The lads walked under the garden,  
They began to sing,  
And the girl felt sorry,  
And began to cry.  

7. – Oh girl, dear girl,  
What faith do you have?  
Why didn’t you cry then  
When I went to war?  

8. Why didn’t you cry then  
Or wail,  
You would have begged for the young man  
To be released from the army.  

9. – Oh brother-comrade,  
You are my comrade,  
On whom, comrade,  
Shall I leave my girl?  

10. – Oh on me, comrade,  
On me, on me,  
And I will take care of her  
Even better than you.  

11. – Take care of me, comrade,  
Take care of me, girl,  
When I return from the army –  
We will drink some horilka.  

12. The Cossack returns from the army,  
Enters the house,  
And the girl’s little child  
Is learning to crawl.  

13. – May you take care of it,  
O evil hour,  
Where did the girl get  
A little child?  

14. The geese flew in from the ford,  
And all are gray, gray,  
Who does not know love,  
Is happy with God.  

15. The geese flew in from the ford,  
And all are white, white,  
Our love is only  
Two weeks old.

1. Oh, if only it were not frost  
And not winter dew,  
I would run  
Barefoot to March.*  
2. And in March  
Two bright little moons,  
There traveled  
Two handsome young men.  
3. They traveled there  
And spoke quietly:  
– Somewhere we should  
Find ourselves a girl?  
4. Let us raise, brothers,  
A viburnum arrow,  
We will fight  
For the dark-haired woman.  
5. Popovich rises  
On a high grave,  
The widower’s son rises  
In a wide valley.  
6. Popovich shot,  
Shot across the valley,  
The widower’s son shot,  
Struck right in the heart.  
7. – [Oh, this to you,]  
Popovich, the girl  
Pierced you  
With a viburnum arrow.  

*Note: "Березень" is the Ukrainian word for March.

1. My dear sent me  
To plant the beans,  
But he himself went to the tavern  
To drink with my godmother.  
2. I planted the beans  
And am going home,  
I turn towards the tavern —  
My dear is drinking with my godmother.  
3. Oh, I came to the tavern,  
Stopped at the doorway,  
Young woman was drenched  
With small tears.  
4. – Don’t cry, dear, at the doorway,  
But go home,  
Cook us some dinner —  
We will come with the godmother.  
5. Oh, I came home,  
Lit the stove,  
Walked around here and there,  
Cooked the dinner.  
6. Evening is falling,  
The moon is rising,  
My dear and the godmother  
Are walking around the yard.  
7. – My wife, my darling,  
Open the door,  
Let me bring my dear godmother  
On white linen.  
8. I let them eat dinner,  
Gave them no spoon,  
Sat down myself, leaning back,  
Cried a little.  
9. They lay down to sleep,  
Started to speak:  
– Now I will be with my dear,  
Now I will beat her.  
10. – Don’t beat, godfather, my dear!  
Why would you beat her?  
I don’t want to and won’t  
Love you anymore.

1. In the garden, a periwinkle,  
It spreads low along the ground.  
Oh, I hear among the people,  
That my beloved is getting married.  
2. – Harness the horse, brother,  
The black horse,  
And let's go to the next village  
To my dear one.  
3. When we arrived at the village, –  
I did not feel happy,  
When we came to the courtyard, –  
My beloved was having a wedding.  
4. His wife Tetyana  
Was standing on the doorstep,  
Leaning on the window,  
Sorrowfully saddened.  
5. – Mikhailo, my man,  
Something came to us:  
Oh, is it family, oh, or strangers,  
Or your elder sister?  
6. – My wife Tetyana,  
My beloved heart,  
It is neither family nor strangers,  
It is my first wife.  
7. In the garden, a periwinkle,  
It has a yellow flower.  
– Why did you not say, why did you not say,  
That you already have a wife?  
8. In the garden, a periwinkle,  
It has blue flowers.  
– Why did you not say, why did you not say,  
That you already have children?  
9. – My wife Tetyana,  
My beloved heart,  
Comb your braid, weave a ribbon,  
Keep going as you have been going.

1. – Leave, Yasusya, your mother,  
I will leave the children,  
Let's go to Ukraine  
To see our fates.  
2. – How can we, Kasia,  
Leave the children?  
You have a husband, –  
He will come after us.  
3. For my unloved one  
I will prepare a potion,  
I will send the old man  
A mournful wedding.

1. And in our country  
The news happened:  
Kateryna was persuading  
The widow's son:*

2. – Leave, Yasiu, your mother,  
I will leave the children  
And we will go to Ukraine  
To look after our fate.

3. – Oh, how will we, Katri,  
Travel around,  
You have Semen at home,  
He will catch up.

4. – Oh, don’t be afraid, Yasiu,  
To travel with me,  
Even early in the morning my Semen  
Went to plow.

5. Semen is plowing, plowing,  
Looking at the road:  
All the women are carrying lunch,  
My beloved has it.

6. Semen is plowing, plowing  
All the way to the crossing,  
He unharnesses the gray oxen  
And goes home.

7. Oh, when Semen arrived  
At the new house:  
– My children are so little,  
But where is your mother?

8. – Our mother went  
Into the forest for viburnum,  
She said to us something:  
That she was leaving us.

9. Our mother went  
Into the forest for calves,  
She said to us something,  
That we are orphans.

1. Oh, by the ford  
A girl was drawing water,  
Two shoemakers met her,  
Handsome in appearance.  

2. – Good evening, girl,  
Drawing cold water,  
Show us the master,  
Where we might spend the night.  

3. – Oh, at the end of the village,  
There is a good master:  
An old craftsman, a young lad,  
The master's young wife.  

4. – Good evening, master,  
Oh, how are you?  
We have come, the servants have come,  
If you have work for us.  

5. The master set them to  
Cutting out soles,  
While he himself went to the tavern  
To party all night.  

6. The shoemakers cut and cut,  
Cutting by starlight,  
Quietly glancing  
At the young master's wife.  

7. – Young master's wife!  
You have much money, –  
Travel with us, the young,  
Leave the old man.  

8. No one heard that,  
Only the master's lad, –  
He cries out, runs to the tavern:  
– My father is here somewhere!  

9. Good evening, father!  
You are drinking vodka here,  
My mother – your wife,  
Has gone traveling.  

10. – Saddle the horse, boy,  
The black horse,  
And for me, the old man, –  
The dun-colored one.  

11. The master caught up with her  
On the cedar bridge:  
– Oh, return, young master’s wife,  
At least visit me.  

12. The master caught up with her  
On the quiet Danube,  
She answered him:  
– I do not know you.  

13. When the master caught her  
By the white sides (of the boat),  
He took and threw the young wife  
Into the deep Danube.  

14. Oh, the young master’s wife floats,  
Like a boat on the water:  
– Save me, my darling, –  
I am your dove!

1. Oh, on the hill  
A tall grave,  
There a girl  
Was cooking dinner:  

2. – Boil, boil,  
Dinner slowly,  
Somewhere my dear  
Is on a distant road.  

3. And already my  
Dinner is boiling over,  
And my dear  
Is stepping on the doorstep.  

4. – Sit down, dear,  
On the hay-covered doorstep  
And tell me,  
What did you hear on the road?  

5. – I heard, darling,  
News about you,  
That you have  
A small child.  

6. – Oh, I had  
A little gypsy girl,  
She left  
A child with me.  

7. – You lie, darling,  
Those are not gypsy eyes,  
It is, darling,  
It looks like you.  

8. Until midnight  
The whip kept cracking,  
And at midnight  
My darling froze.

1. Oh, on the mountain  
A high grave,*  
There a girl  
Was cooking dinner,  
Before dinner  
She spoke quietly:  

2. – Boil, boil,  
Dinner, quietly,  
There is no  
My dear fiancé.  

3. And already my  
Dinner is boiling,  
And my dear  
Is stepping on the doorstep.  

4. – Sit down, dear,  
On the hay-covered doorstep,  
And tell me,  
What did you hear on the road?  

5. – I heard, dear,  
News about you,  
That you had  
A little child.  

6. – Yesterday a  
Gypsy girl was in the house  
And left  
This child on the bench.  

7. – You lie, dear,  
You lie, dark-browed one,  
Because a gypsy's  
Child is dark,  
And the suckling  
Is white as paper.  

8. – Go, dear,  
To the hut to sleep,  
I will tell you  
The whole truth.  

9. That for two nights,  
Two dark nights,  
A young lad  
Stayed with me.  

10. And on the third  
Dark night  
He left me  
A little child.  

11. Until midnight  
The dear one begged,  
And at midnight  
The whip cracked,  
At dawn  
The dear one became numb.  

12. I would give, I would give  
A golden chest,  
To wake up  
My dear lady.  

13. I would give, I would give  
A cow with a calf,  
To wake up  
My dear to the child.  

*Note: “Висока могила” literally means "high grave," but in some contexts it can refer to a high hill or mound.

1. I walk through the garden  
Planting a cherry tree.  
– Come here, my dear,  
I have something to tell you.

2. Come to the room, –  
I am going to die.  
Oh, will you, my dear,  
Mourn for me?

3. My dear became sad.  
Went to shave himself:  
– If I could take the body from the house,  
I would get married.

4. Oh, the girls came  
To take the body away,  
And my dear is winking:  
Which one should I take?

5. If only to take such a one,  
Who would work well,  
Whose face is rosy  
And eyebrows dark.

6. The children heard that  
And began to cry:  
– Our dear mother,  
Where can we find you now?

7. Oh, you can’t be bought,  
You can’t be earned...  
Let’s hold hands  
And go to serve.

8. The mother heard this,  
Started to cry:  
– Quiet, children, don’t cry,  
I have just gone to sleep.

9. – My dear, my dear,  
How unfaithful you are!  
Oh, I thought you were dead,  
But you have come back to life!

1. In the cherry garden, the orchard,
The nightingale was chirping,
I quietly approach the house,
There the father-in-law was reading a letter.*
2. – Come here, daughter-in-law, listen,
To what my son has written to you.
He writes that he is already married,
He wrote you "forget me."
3. The daughter-in-law said nothing,
She said just one word:
I would soon forget him,
If it were not for his child.
4. – Oh lullaby, my son, lullaby,
Oh lullaby, it’s time for you to sleep.
And I will go sit by the table
To write a letter to my father.
5. She wrote a letter, wrote,
She was finishing writing.
Come soon, my dear, home,
To bury me, a young woman.
6. Three Sundays have already passed
Since the father-in-law buried the daughter-in-law.
Then the son sends a letter,
Saying that he was just joking.
7. The young heart stopped beating,
The young blood stopped flowing,
He remembers the woman and the child,
He remembers his first love.

1. Oh, from beneath the deep bridge  
A tall maple tree grew.  
2. From beneath that maple  
A young widow came out.  
3. The young widow came out  
And gave birth to two sons.  
4. She gave birth to two sons,  
Wrapped them in white calico.  
5. She wrapped them in white calico  
And placed them on a ship.  
6. She placed them on a ship  
And cast it into the sea.  
7. – Oh, you quiet water,  
Take my two sons.  
8. Take my two sons:  
Ivanyusha and Vasyl.  
9. You, yellow sand,  
Feed my children.  
10. Twenty-five years passed,  
The widow went out to fetch water.  
11. The widow went out to fetch water,  
And rain began to drizzle.  
12. The rain began to drizzle,  
The Danube began to rise.  
13. The Danube began to rise,  
The ship began to sail.  
14. And on that ship  
Were two young sailors.  
15. One sits in front,  
Looking over the water.  
16. The other sits behind,  
Looking at the widow.  
17. – Oh, you young widow,  
Will you not marry us both?  
18. Will you not marry us both,  
Ivanyusha and Vasyl?  
19. – I will not marry you both,  
I promise my daughter to Ivanyusha.  
20. – Oh, you young widow,  
Then who are you?  
21. – I am a townsman's daughter,  
With the surname Karpynka.  
22. And you, young sailor,  
Who are you?  
23. – I am a townsman,  
Called Karpiv’s son.  
24. – Oh our God, our God,  
What kind of world is this now?  
25. What kind of world is this now  
That a son does not recognize his mother?  
26. That a son does not recognize his mother,  
And brother married sister.

1. Oh, far, far away  
The blackthorn grows tall.  
2. And from beneath that blackthorn  
A young widow emerged.  
3. The young widow came out  
And led out two sons.  
4. She led out two sons:  
Mykityusha and Vasyl.  
5. Mykityusha and Vasyl  
She threw into the quiet Danube:  
6. – Oh, you clear water,  
Take my two sons.  
7. The water says: – I will take them.  
The wave says: – I will throw them out.  
8. The wave says: – I will throw them out.  
The sun says: – I will warm them.  
9. The sun says: – I will warm them.  
The wind says: – I will blow on them.  
10. – You, tall reed,  
Give the children supper.  
11. You, tall sedge,  
Give the children milk.  
12. A ship came sailing  
And spoke to the widow:  
13. – Oh, widow, oh widow,  
Will you love us two?  
14. – For one I will go,  
For the other I give my daughter.  
15. – Oh, widow, oh widow,  
Your head is foolish.  
16. Your head is foolish:  
First you drowned your sons.  
17. First you drowned your sons,  
Now you would be friends.

1. The glorious town of Lebedyn,  
Yes, the glorious town of Lebedyn,  
Lebedyn,  
In which I...  
In which I fought long ago.  

2. In which I fought long ago,  
Yes, in which I fought long ago...  
Long ago I fought,  
Earned seven hundred rubles...  
Earned seven hundred rubles.  

3. Earned seven hundred rubles,  
Yes, earned seven hundred rubles...  
Seven hundred rubles earned,  
In a day and a night...  
In a day and a night I spent it all.  

4. But one hundred rubles remained,  
Yes, one hundred rubles remained...  
One hundred rubles,  
Having hired a troika...  
Having hired a troika of horses.  

5. Having hired a troika of horses,  
Yes, having hired a troika...  
A troika of horses:  
“Drive on, brother...  
Drive on, brother, faster.”  

6. Drive on, brother, faster,  
Yes, drive on, brother, hey, faster,  
To the young innkeeper woman...  
To the young innkeeper woman.  

7. “Young innkeeper woman,  
Yes, young innkeeper woman,  
Hey, hey, young one,  
Serve beer...  
Serve beer and also some wine.”  

8. “But I won’t serve beer,  
Yes, I won’t serve beer,  
Hey, hey, I won’t serve,  
Because you, Vanya,  
Because you, Vanya, are a charlatan.”  

9. “Oh, maybe I am a charlatan,  
Yes, oh, maybe I am,  
Hey, hey, a charlatan,  
I have...  
I have a pitcher of money.”  

10. “If you have a pitcher of money,  
Yes, if you have,  
Hey, hey, a pitcher of money,  
I will give my daughter to you.”  

11. I will give my daughter to you,  
Yes, I will give,  
Hey, hey, give my daughter  
[And in addition...]  
Also a servant girl.  

12. “The devil take you and your daughter,  
Yes, the devil take you,  
Hey, hey, and your daughter,  
And in addition...  
And in addition the servant girl.”

1. Oh, the moon has risen, risen bright,  
A mist lies in the valley.  
There beyond the valley is a hut,  
And in that hut, a fire is burning.  

2. – The Cossack will not return anymore, daughter,  
He lies somewhere in the damp earth...  
It seems such is fate,  
That yours was not destined to be.  

3. She put towels on another,  
Untied her fair braid,  
Wiped away small tears:  
She was deciding her own fate.

1. The moon rolled out from behind the cloud  
And lit up the whole valley.  
Having lit up the whole valley,  
It rolled back behind the clouds again.  

2. And in that valley stands a house,  
In that house a little fire burns,  
There a mother teaches her daughter,  
The daughter sits tearful.  

3. – Oh, my beautiful, dear daughter,  
You are my only daughter,  
Do not wait, do not wait for the Cossack from the battle,  
He is no longer alive.  

4. Many have been killed,  
Many are no longer here,  
Many girls have gotten married,  
But why do you sit alone?  

5. On the third evening, everything became silent,  
Neither mother nor daughter could be heard,  
And on the fourth, the girl was giving towels to the unloved one.  

6. She gave them, wiped away her tears,  
And made a vow to her beloved,  
That she would either love another,  
Or go to the grave alone,  
To love another beloved,  
Or to go to the grave alive.

1. Oh, in the garden, oh, in the garden,  
Under the apple tree,  
There sat a dove  
With a dove.  
2. An eagle flew in  
From a black cloud,  
He broke apart  
The pair of doves,  
3. Oh, he killed the dove,  
Left the dove,  
And took the dove  
As his own beloved.  
4. He sprinkles millet  
And wheat for the dove;  
Millet and wheat,  
Pours water.  
5. – Oh, eat, little dove,  
Oh, drink, little dove,  
Oh, drink, little dove,  
You are my beloved.  
6. – I will not eat,  
I will not drink, –  
There is no dove –  
There is no one to live with.  
7. Thirty-seven pairs  
Of doves are flying,  
Choose for yourself  
Which dove is yours.  
8. – Let them fly  
Thirty-seven pairs, –  
There is none like  
My dove was.  
9. My dove  
Has silver wings,  
Near his heart  
Golden feathers.

1. Oh, under the mountain,  
Under the stone,  
There sat a dove  
With a dove.*  
2. They kissed,  
They admired each other,  
With bluish wings  
They embraced.  
3. Oh, somewhere appeared  
A shooter from behind the mountain,  
He killed the dove,  
And caught the female dove.  
4. He brought her to the room,  
Scattering wheat:  
– Eat and drink,  
Young widow.  
5. – How can I eat,  
How can I drink, –  
The world is so wide,  
But there is no one to live with.  
6. – Oh, I have  
Seven hundred doves, –  
Choose for yourself  
Whichever you like.  
7. – Oh, let it be  
Seven hundred and four, –  
There is no one  
Who was my dear.  
8. And my dear –  
Black-browed,  
With a rosy face,  
And very young.  
9. When he speaks,  
It rings like a bell,  
When he laughs –  
The garden blossoms.  
10. Those paths  
Have overgrown,  
Where my dear’s feet  
Used to walk.  
11. Overgrown with  
Thorny grass,  
Where we walked,  
My heart, with you.

1. Oh, a young girl was walking along the shore

2. Oh, where did a lively young man come from,

He killed, killed the best

1. Oh, on the pond,  
Oh, on the pond, on the little pond, on the water,  
There swam little swans, swans.  
2. Oh, somewhere appeared,  
Oh, somewhere appeared a brave young marksman,  
3. He fired  
And hit the finest swan.  
4. It scattered,  
White feathers scattered on the water.  
5. Poured out,  
Red blood poured on the grass.  
6. Oh, that blood,  
Red blood – is dew on the grass.  
7. Oh, those feathers,

Oh, I will sit by the edge of the steppe, Hey,  
Oh, I will sit by the edge of the steppe,  
To look out for the Black Sea Cossack.

1. Oh Vasyl, dear Vasyl,  
You are a child of the people,  
Riding across the gray field on a horse, –  
To gaze lovingly.  
2. Oh, the horse runs, the earth trembles,  
The horse senses the road,  
Oh God knows, God is aware,  
Where Vasyl spends the night.  
3. Oh, Vasyl spends the night  
In a deep valley,  
He tied the horse to a maple  
Among red viburnum.  
4. Good it is for the horse  
To stand among the red viburnum,  
But even better for Vasyl  
To lie on soft bedding.  
5. Good it is for the horse  
To stand among the viburnum,  
But even better for Vasyl  
To come for supper.  
6. – Come, come, dear Vasyl,  
Come for supper,  
If you don’t come for supper,  
Come for lunch.  
7. If you don’t come for supper,  
Come for lunch,  
If you don’t come for lunch,  
Come to visit.  
8. If you don’t come to visit,  
It will be bad for you:  
You will drown, you will drown in the sand,  
Where there is no water.  
9. On Sunday early morning  
I came to fetch water,  
Vasyl is drifting, drifting,  
With no one to save him.  
10. – Here, fishermen,  
Four coins each, –  
Save Vasyl,  
For he is a good lad.  
11. – Here, fishermen,  
Half a gold coin each,  
Save Vasyl,  
Even if he is lifeless.  
12. Vasyl is drifting, drifting  
In the valley with his shoulders,  
Behind him, behind him a girl  
With black eyes.  
13. They carried Vasyl  
Between the mountains,  
They dragged him against the water  
With gray oxen.*

1. – Oh, I told you, girl,  
That you would not grieve,  
When I sit down and leave  
To serve the sovereign. *  
2. Oh, the Cossack rode out  
Beyond the new gates;  
Neither sleep nor work  
Could take the girl.  
3. Oh, the Cossack rode out  
Beyond the yellow sands;  
Running, running after the Cossack  
Three messengers went on foot.  
4. – Oh, return, Cossack,  
For your father is dying.  
– I will not return – I will entertain myself,  
Let him rest with God.  
5. – Oh, return, Cossack,  
For your mother is dying.  
– I will not return – I will entertain myself,  
Let her rest with God.  
6. – Oh, return, Cossack,  
For the girl is dying.  
– I must hasten on horseback  
To find her alive.  
7. Oh, the Cossack arrived  
At her yard:  
All the bells rang  
At the sorrowful wedding.  
8. Oh, the Cossack arrived  
At the new chamber, –  
The girl lies, lies  
Across the entire table. **  
9. – My little white legs,  
You folded yourselves,  
When I rode off to the soldier,  
You came out with me.  
10. And my little white hands,  
You also folded yourselves,  
When I rode off to the soldier,  
You bade me farewell.  
11. My lovely lips,  
You wrote yourself down, –  
When I rode off to the soldier,  
You spoke with me.  
12. My dark eyes,  
You closed yourselves,  
When I rode off to the soldier,  
You looked at me.  
13. My dark eyebrows,  
Blink at me,  
When I rode off to the soldier, –  
You blinked at me.

1. – A little cherry and a little sour cherry,  
Small berries,  
Why did we fall in love,  
So young?  
2. We fell in love, we fell in love  
With heavy sorrow,  
You stayed at home,  
And I will go further.  
3. Will you not, my dear,  
Be sad for me,  
When I sit down and leave  
To serve the tsar?  
4. – Oh, I will not, my darling,  
I will not, I will not,  
You go to the garrison, I to another,  
I will forget about you.  
5. As soon as the young recruit left  
Beyond the thick vines,  
His beloved shed  
Small tears.  
6. As soon as the young recruit left  
To the garrison, the garrison,  
They carry his beloved, dark-browed,  
Lifeless in their arms.  
7. As soon as the young recruit left  
To the yellow sands,  
He looks back at himself, –  
Runs and walks on foot.  
8. – Oh, come back, young recruit,  
Mother is dying.  
– I will not return, I will stay,  
Let her rest in peace.  
9. – Oh, come back, young recruit,  
Beloved is dying,  
Oh, come back, young recruit,  
Beloved is dying.  
10. Oh, as the raven neighs like a horse,  
I have nowhere to stand,  
(To) catch my dark-browed beloved  
Still alive.  
11. Oh, the young recruit arrived  
Under the new gates,  
Lies the dark-browed beloved,  
Better than silver and gold.  
12. – You said, my dear,  
You would not be sad,  
But you go, young one,  
To rot in damp earth.

1. – The cherry is not a sour cherry,  
The berries are small,  
But why have we fallen in love,  
So young as we are?*  
2. But we fell in love, yes, we fell in love,  
In heavy sorrow,  
You remain like a dove,  
And I will go on further.  
3. But what will you do, dear girl,  
When you miss me,  
When I sit down and go  
To serve the tsar?  
4. – Oh, I will not grieve,  
I will not, I will not,  
You go one way, I another,  
I will forget you.  
5. Oh, the young Cossack rode out  
Beyond the new gates,  
But neither sleep nor work  
Would take the girl.  
6. Oh, the young Cossack rode out  
Beyond the dark forests,  
And three messengers are running, running  
On foot after the Cossack.  
7. – Oh, come back, young Cossack,  
Your father is dying.  
– I will not return, I will stay,  
They rest with God.  
8. – Oh, come back, young Cossack,  
The girl is dying.  
– One must hurry on horseback  
To catch her alive.  
9. Oh, the young Cossack arrived  
At the new gates,  
And the girl lies, lies  
On a wide bench.  
10. – You said, dear girl,  
You would not grieve,  
But now you will, because of me,  
Rot in the cold earth.

1. The guelder-rose bent down,  
The young girl  
Was sorrowful.*  
2. The young girl  
Was sorrowful,  
Because she was born  
Unfortunate.  
3. Because she was born  
Unfortunate,  
She went to the blue sea –  
And drowned.  
4. She went to the blue sea –  
And drowned,  
And her mother  
Was grieving for her.  
5. And her mother  
Kept lamenting:  
“No one is saving  
My daughter.”  
6. “No need to save her,  
I will be here,  
I will not forget  
Whom I truly love.”  
7. Whom I loved  
Faithfully, –  
It is hard  
To forget them.  
8. When the Cossack arrived  
From Ukraine,  
He tied his horse  
To the guelder-rose.  
9. “Wide grave,  
Open up,  
Young girl,  
Answer me.”  
10. “Young girl,  
Answer me:  
Will you allow me  
To marry?”  
11. “Marry, Cossack,  
In Petrivka,  
Do not take a widow,  
Take a maiden.”  
12. The maiden will  
Respect you,  
For your first husband  
Is your mother.

1. In the valley on the hunt,  
A girl is walking;  
Her beloved came there,  
Sat down and said:  

2. – Oh, stop, my dear,  
Stop walking,  
Come, sit beside me –  
I have something to say.  

3. Oh, I will then say  
But what do I know...  
Oh, my dear,  
I have to leave you.  

4. – Tell me, my darling,  
What is the reason,  
Do you not want me yourself,  
Or is it your family?  

5. – Oh, I truly love you,  
My family does not forbid,  
Only that I drink and wander around –  
That’s what your family says.  

6. Oh, I will go to Ukraine  
And live there,  
I will find myself a better beloved,  
Who will love me.  

7. The boy died without reason,  
One must pound a stone...  
She has left and did not want  
The girl to love.

1. Red rose,  
Yellow saffrons,  
Heavy on the heart,  
Evening will come.  

2. As the Cossack was going  
To serve in the army,  
He had to leave  
The girl alone.  

3. A year is passing,  
The second is ending,  
There is no news  
From the young Cossack.  

4. Here the Cossack  
Returns from the campaign  
And asks people  
About the girl.  

5. – We had a girl,  
Eyes like blackthorn,  
She waited for the Cossack, –  
It has been a year since she died.  

6. Oh, in the middle of the field  
A poplar bends...  
Oh, heavy-heavy  
Is the Cossack’s fate.

1. Oh, the young Cossack rode out  
To the steep mountain,  
Leaving his wife Maryana  
Adorned with her charms.  

2. Oh, the young Cossack rode out  
To go hunting,  
Set his black horse loose  
In the green grass.  

3. Set his black horse loose  
In the green grass,  
And he himself went to lie down and sleep,  
For he was filled with sorrow.  

4. Oh, the Cossack slept,  
He dreamed a dream,  
That from the right sleeve  
A falcon flew out.  

5. Whether from the right or the left  
The gray bird,  
It knows and finds out  
What is happening at home.  

6. Oh, as the young Cossack rose  
On his white feet,  
He ran from the mountain to the valley  
To the fortune teller.  

7. – Fortune teller, diviner,  
Guess this dream for me,  
That from the right sleeve  
A falcon flew out.  

8. – And I, young Cossack,  
Will interpret your dream,  
How could I not guess it?  
I will stake my head on it.  

9. Your wife Maryana  
Has given birth to a son,  
Only two hours left to daybreak,  
Her life has ended.  

10. When the young Cossack rode out  
To the steep mountain,  
There was no place to send her,  
Except to his home.  

11. When the young Cossack arrived  
Under the gate:  
– Come out, wife Maryana,  
Open the gates.  

12. Oh, Maryana did not come out,  
Out came her mother:  
– Greetings, greetings, young Cossack,  
You are no longer my son-in-law.  

13. Oh, when the young Cossack entered  
His own house,  
His faithful servants  
Were all in mourning.  

14. Oh, when the young Cossack entered  
The new chamber,  
His wife Maryana lay  
Dead as a stone.  

15. – Oh, get up, wife Maryana,  
Oh, get up, don’t lie down,  
The little son cried,  
Get up, and amuse him.

1. Oh, the young Cossack rode out  
To war,  
Leaving his wife Maryana,  
Like a painting.*  
2. Oh, the young Cossack rode out  
To a steep hill,  
Brought his black horse  
Into the silky grass.  
3. Brought his black horse  
Into the silky grass,  
And he himself went and lay down to sleep  
In a wide valley.  
4. Oh, the young Cossack is sleeping,  
He dreams a dream,  
That from his right sleeve  
A falcon flew out.  
5. That from his right sleeve  
A falcon flew out,  
And from the left, from under the white –  
A gray bird.  
6. Oh, the young Cossack got up suddenly  
On his white feet,  
Ran, ran, the young Cossack  
Right to the fortune-teller.  
7. – Fortune-teller, little seer,  
Interpret my dream,  
That from my right sleeve  
A falcon flew out.  
8. That from my right sleeve  
A falcon flew out,  
And from the left, from under the white –  
A gray bird.  
9. – Young Cossack,  
I will tell you,  
If your dream does not come true,  
I will lay down my head.  
10. Your wife Maryana  
Has given birth to a son,  
At the third hour past midnight  
Has passed away.  
11. Oh, the young Cossack rode out  
To the steep hill,  
No light can be seen anywhere,  
Except at his home.  
12. Oh, the young Cossack came  
To the white house,  
Maryana lies  
On the whole bed.  
13. – Oh, get up, wife Maryana,  
Oh, get up, don’t lie down,  
When the little son cries,  
Take him in your arms.  
14. – Oh, I would be glad, my dear,  
Oh, I would be glad to get up,  
But the damp earth has covered my breast,  
I cannot rise.

1. Oh, if I had known  
When that death would come,  
I wouldn't have drunk vodka,  
I would have gone among the people.  
2. I would have told myself  
To cut down the sycamore,  
To cut down the sycamore,  
To build a coffin.  
3. That coffin lies  
For three foolish years,  
That coffin lies  
For three foolish years.  
4. It lay there and lay,  
It began to rot,  
It began to rot,  
It began to speak:  
5. – Either lay someone in me,  
Or burn me,  
Either lay someone in me,  
Or burn me.  
6. I would have laid my father,  
But the house is not for the father,  
I would have laid my mother,  
But the mother is old.  
7. I would lay my beloved,  
I won’t find another.  
I would lay my beloved,  
I won’t find another.  
8. Oh, I would lie down myself,  
Young on my own,  
Young on my own –  
A little child.  
9. – Lie down, my dear, lie down,  
It will somehow be,  
|: And people will raise

1. Oh, if I had known  
That death would come to me,  
I would have already said  
To cut down the maple.*  
2. I would have already said  
To cut down the maple,  
To cut down the maple –  
To build a coffin.  
3. Oh, the coffin stood  
For twenty-four years,  
Twenty-five is passing –  
The coffin speaks.  
4. Twenty-five is passing –  
The coffin speaks:  
“Either place something inside,  
Or take it apart.”  
5. “I would place my mother –  
There would be no order,  
I would place my father –  
Father is no good.”  
6. I would place my brother –  
Brother has a new house,  
Brother has a new house –  
A great loss.  
7. I would place my sister –  
Sister has small children,  
Sister has small children –  
Orphans forever.  
8. I would place my father-in-law –  
Father-in-law does not die,  
I would place my mother-in-law –  
The earth does not accept.  
9. I would place my beloved –  
There is no such one,  
There is no such one  
Among my young men.  
10. I would lie down, young as I am –  
But I fear the grave,  
Because I am very young  
And a small child.  
11. “Lie down, lie down, dear –  
Somehow it will be,  
The little child  
Will be raised by people.”  
12. “May it be, my dear,  
Heavy to die,  
Oh, how hard it is for me

1. Oh, if only I had known

2. I would have told myself  
To cut down the maple,  
To cut down the maple,  
To build a coffin.

Built the coffin,  
The craftsmen foolishly  
Built the coffin.

1. Oh, I am weak, my dear,  
I see that I will die!  
So build, my dear,  
A cedar coffin.  
2. – But where will you, my dear,  
Get the cedar wood?  
You will, you will, my dear,  
Lie in an oak coffin!

My silk shirt.  
4. – But where will you, my dear,  
Get the silk?  
You will, you will, my dear,  
Lie in white!  
5. – You will bury me, my dear,  
In a cherry orchard,  
And you will cover, my dear,  
The little grave with earth.  
6. And you will cover, my dear,  
The high grave,  
And you will plant, my dear,  
A red viburnum.

To eat that viburnum,

Give your daughter away,

Give your son in marriage,

1. Oh, you long autumn,

1. Oh, on the mountain a pine tree,

Oh, drinks and strolls,

Plays the violin.

Plays the violin,

Encourages Handzia:

* "Бігме" is an oath or exclamation roughly equivalent to "By God" or "Indeed."

Steel above steel,

1. On the Japanese shore  
The sailors stood,

Was walking with a sailor.*  

2. – Sailors, oh sailors,

Got on, left...  

8. One year passed, two years passed,  
They have a big family,  
They will call their son a sailor’s:  
Dear grandmother.  

9. – Come, dear daughter,  
With whom you had advice,  
You did not want  
To listen to my advice.

1. A Cossack was riding through the valley  
And met Halyna.  
2. – Oh, stop, Halya,  
I have something to tell you.  
3. I have something to tell you:  
I will ask you seven riddles.  
4. If you guess them – you will be mine;  
If you don’t guess – you will be someone else’s.  
5. – Oh, as if I were foolish –  
I did not guess seven riddles.  
6. – Oh, what lights up a steep mountain?  
Oh, what runs without acceleration?  
7. Oh, what rises without seed?  
Oh, what grows without roots?  
8. Oh, what combs the dew on the braid?  
Oh, what cries without a voice?  
9. Oh, what curls around the tree?  
Oh, what burns near the heart?  
10. The girl stood still, thought, –  
Guessed all seven riddles:  
11. – The moon lights the steep mountain,  
Water runs without acceleration.  
12. The sun rises without seed,  
The stone grows without roots.  
13. The girl combs the dew on the braid,  
The dew cries without a voice.  
14. And hops curls around the tree,  
Love burns near the heart.  
15. Now the girl became mine, –  
She guessed all seven riddles.

1. – Young Cossack,  
Why are you sad?  
Have the oxen stopped,  
Or have you lost your way?  

2. – The oxen have not stopped,  
I have not lost my way,  
I loved a girl, –  
I am deeply sorrowful.  

3. I loved a girl,  
But I do not have her.  
Come out, girl,  
From the green grove.  

4. The Cossack went to the highland,  
The girl to the lowland...  
Oh, the Cossack became a thorn bush,  
The girl a guelder rose bush.  

5. The Cossack’s mother  
Went to pick thorn bushes,  
The girl’s mother –  
To break guelder rose branches.  

6. Oh, that is not a thorn bush, –  
That is your little son;  
Oh, that is not a guelder rose –  
That is your child.  

7. – Saddle up, Cossack,  
The black horse,  
We’ll sit and ride  
To the stranger priest.  

8. Oh, we arrived –  
The priest was not home:  
Is it your misfortune,  
Or my sorrow?  

9. Saddle up, Cossack,  
The black horse –  
We’ll sit and ride  
Into the open field.  

10. We’ll sit and ride  
Into the open field,  
Oh, there the girl  
Is weeding the wheat field.  

11. The wheat field,  
She is pulling out darnel,  
Throwing it  
Before the black horse’s feet.  

12. – Oh, stop, girl,  
Weeding the wheat,  
Weeding the wheat –  
Pulling out the darnel.  

13. Weeding the wheat,  
Pulling out the darnel,  
Throwing it  
Before the black horse’s feet.  

14. Oh, stop, girl,  
Pulling out the darnel –  
Spread a white bed,  
We will talk.  

15. We will talk,  
We will speak...  
The girl is afraid,  
Lest she betray.  

16. – Don’t be afraid, girl,  
Don’t be afraid of anything –  
The young Cossack  
Has betrayed no one.  

17. Oh, the girl  
Fell soundly asleep,  
The Cossack betrayed her,  
So she did not even hear.

1. – Don’t go, Cossack,  
On dark nights,  
And don’t wither my little heart  
With your dark eyebrows.*  
2. – I have dark eyebrows,  
I do not grieve.  
If girls do not want to love me, –  
I will go and drown myself.  
3. I will go and drown myself  
In deep pits...  
Oh, will the girls and young women  
Mourn for me?  
4. – Don’t drown yourself, Cossack,  
You will lose your soul in vain...  
Tell me, tell me the true truth:  
Whom do you love?  
5. – I love a girl,  
God punish me!  
My heart cannot  
Breathe without yours.  
6. – Saddle up, Cossack,  
The black horse,  
And let’s go get married  
At a stranger priest’s.  
7. Oh, we rode across the field  
And then the second one,  
But on the third step –  
Our horse stumbled.  
8. We arrived at the priest’s, –  
The priest was not home;  
Either your fate or mine  
Is unhappy.  
9. Let’s go, Cossack,  
To the new house,  
So the old mother won’t hear,  
We will talk.  
10. You will sit on the bench,  
And I on the donkey,  
And we will watch carefully  
Who has dark eyebrows.  
11. I have dark eyebrows  
And you, like a jackdaw,  
We will go out into the street  
Like a flock of pigeons.

1. – Don’t go, Cossack,

With your black eyebrows.*  
2. – I have black eyebrows,

If the girls don’t want to love me, –

3. I will go, I will drown myself

8. We arrived at the priest’s, –

9. Let’s go, Cossack,

10. You sit on the bench,

11. I have black eyebrows

1. – Oh, girl,  
Oh, proud, splendid one,  
Why didn’t you come out to me  
Since the evening?  

2. – Oh, how can I come out to you,  
Close enemies  
Will judge us.  

3. – Let them judge  
The thoughtless ones –  
I love you  
Like my own health.  

4. Oh, girl, –  
With a quiet step,  
I love you,  
As you walk by the water.  

5. Not as by the water,  
But as you go with the water, –  
Dear girl,  
I want to talk with you.  

6. Oh, girl,  
You walk like a duck,  
Oh, step, step  
Onto that boat quickly.  

7. As soon as the girl  
Stepped onto the boat,  
There appeared  
A swift lynx.  

8. There appeared  
A swift lynx  
And the young girl  
Drowned.  

9. Here drowned  
A girl from Podolia,  
Only a red kerchief  
Floated up.  

10. – Oh, who will  
Save me?  
That one will  
Get paid by my father.  

11. – I wouldn’t want  
Payment from your father,  
I only want to  
Take you as my wife.  

12. – I would rather, boy,  
Drown in this Danube,  
Than have to be  
Your wife.  

13. Oh, the Cossack shouted  
To his wife:  
– Hey, give me  
That long pole.  

14. Oh, give me  
That long pole,  
I will push the treacherous girl  
Far away.

1. – What are you doing,  
My dear mother,  
What are you drinking –  
Why are you giving me away  
To a man I do not love?

2. I do not love,  
I have not loved,  
And I will not,  
I will not faithfully live  
With a man I do not love.

3. Oh, on Sunday,  
Very early –  
The dark-haired girl  
Is getting ready  
To the white world.

4. – Where are you going,  
Dark-haired one,  
Why are you leaving me,  
A young man?

5. – I am going,  
To the unloved one,  
Home.  
– I will saddle  
The black horse,  
And catch up.

6. I will tie you  
To the horse’s mane  
With a braid,  
And drag you  
Like a black thorn.

7. Enough, enough,  
Black horse,  
Stop wandering,  
It is time  
For noon rest.

8. Give me, give me,  
The unloved one,  
A sharp knife,  
I will pull out  
The black thorn  
From white feet.

9. The dark-haired girl  
Was pulling out  
A thorn  
And stabbed  
The sharp knife  
Into the heart.

10. – Stab, stab,  
Sharp knife,  
In blood –  
Woe is me,  
Young woman,  
In a foreign land.

11. Take me back,  
The unloved one,  
To the village –  
Father and mother will rejoice  
That I am alive.

1. – What are you doing,  
My dear mother,  
What are you drinking,  
Why do you  
Give me away  
To an unloving man?***

2. And the unloving man  
Has bitter lips,  
Like wormwood,  
I will not,  
My dear mother,  
Live with him.

3. And on Sunday  
Early in the morning,  
Just at dawn,  
A dark-haired girl  
Set out  
Into the white world.

4. – Travel on, travel on,  
Dark-haired girl,  
I will catch up,  
Tie you  
To a forelock  
With a braid.

5. I will tie you  
To a forelock  
With a braid,  
And drag  
A black hawthorn  
With you.

6. I, on Sunday  
Early in the morning,  
Just at dawn,  
Was picking  
Black hawthorn  
In the white snow.

1. I am in the town of Bohuslav  
At the manor of Pan Kanyovsky,  
There was walking Bondarivna,  
Like a splendid peacock.*  
2. When Pan Kanyovsky arrived,  
And took off his hat, took it off,  
He embraced, he embraced Bondarivna  
And kissed her.  
3. – Oh, you are not allowed, Pan Kanyovsky,  
To kiss me,  
You are only allowed, Pan Kanyovsky,  
To take off my shoes.  
4. And good people whispered softly to Bondarivna:  
– Run away, run away, Bondarivna,  
Trouble will come to you!  
5. Oh, Bondarivna ran away  
Through gardens, through meadows,  
And behind her two Cossacks  
With bare sabers.  
6. And they laid Bondarivna  
On a yew bench,  
While Pan Kanyovsky ordered  
To dig a pit.  
7. Bondarivna lay there, lay  
Still one full day and more,  
Pan Kanyovsky nodded  
To make a coffin from oak.

1. Oh, I was standing under a willow,  
And the girl was under another.  
– Come out, girl,  
Come out, little fish,  
Let me talk to you.  

2. – I would be glad to come out,  
To talk with you, my dear, –  
My darling is lying  
On my right arm  
And I am afraid to wake him.  

3. Oh, you young Cossack,  
Under you is a black horse, –  
Saddle the horse,  
Ride out of the yard, –  
You’re not mine, I’m not yours.  

4. The Cossack saddles his horse  
And says to him:  
– Fly, my horse,  
Fly, black one,  
To the quiet Danube.  

5. Oh, I arrived at the Danube,  
I stand and think:  
Should I kill myself from the horse,  
Or drown myself,  
Or turn back?  

6. I fall from the horse – I won’t die,  
I jump into the Danube – I will drown:  
Eat me, little fish,  
Eat me sharply  
In the quiet Danube.

1. Oh, there is a small lake in the field,  
There floats a little bucket.  
|: Pine staves, oak bottom,  
Come out, come out, my heart. :|  
2. – Oh, I would be glad to come out,  
To speak with you,  
|: The unloved one lies on the right hand,  
And I am afraid to wake him. :|  
3. – Oh, my dear girl,  
Turn away from the unloved one,  
|: Turn away from the unloved one,  
For I will kill him with a rifle, like a dove. :|  
4. – Oh, whether you hit or miss,  
You will not separate me from my pair.  
|: Oh, whether you strike or not,  
You will only shorten my life. :|

1. – You, moon, reshape yourself,  
And come out, come out beyond the mountain.  
|: Come out, girl  
With black eyes,  
Let me talk to you. :|  

2. – Oh, I would be glad to come out  
And talk with you,  
|: But my sweetheart  
Is on my right hand,  
And I am afraid to wake him. :|  

3. – You, my dear girl,  
Turn away from the unloving one,  
|: Dear, the unloving one  
I will strike down like a dove,  
And I will live with you. :|  

4. – Whether you kill or do not kill,  
You will lose your soul from this world in vain,  
|: Saddle your horse,  
Ride out of the yard,  
You are not mine, I am not yours. :|

1. – Moon, I will alter you,  
(Y) come out, come out beyond the mountain.  
|: (Y) come out, girl  
With black eyes,  
Let me talk to you. :|  
2. – Oh, I would be glad to come out  
And talk with you,  
|: (Y) but there is an unlover

1. Oh, my dear one went away  
To China Town  
And my dear one bought  
A new ship.  
2. He paid three and a half kopecks,  
Seated me  
And the child:  
3. – Oh, sail, my dear,  
Oh, sail, black-browed one,  
To the family:  
May a bad hour  
Meet you.  
4. The ship sails,  
The water roars,  
My heart grieves,  
Gnawed like rust.

1. Oh, I will go up the hill,  
I will look into the valley.  
2. I will look into the valley,  
At the red viburnum.  
3. On the viburnum is a nightingale,  
A little nightingale.  
4. – Little nightingale,  
You have a thin voice.  
5. Do not chirp until dawn,  
Do not wither my heart.  
6. Chirp early in the morning,  
Cheer up my little heart.  
7. Chirp for me,  
Because I am poor, in a foreign land.  
8. Because I am poor, in a foreign land –  
I have no kin with me.  
9. I have no family or relatives,  
No faithful friend.  
10. Only one child –  
She embraced me.  
11. She embraced me –  
And bathed me in tears.  
12. – Oh, you swift water,  
You are occupying the shores.  
13. Take my wife away —  
So that I may not live with her.  
14. The wife heard that,  
And fainted on the bench.  
15. He laid her out by the table  
And covered her with a cloth.  
16. He put on his hat and went  
Out to the street to the girls.  
17. All the girls say,  
Only one says nothing.  
18. Is she not the dear sister?  
She cried and left.  
19. – It is not a good day, our father,  
Our son-in-law is outside.  
20. Playing the flute, –  
May the Lord punish him swiftly,  
21. May the Lord punish him,  
Because he has no wife.

1. Ivan walks by the estuary,  
Ivan walks by the estuary,  
And he meets a Turkish lord.  
2. “Ivan, do you catch fish,  
Ivan, do you catch fish,  
And do you ferry people?”  
3. “No, sir, I do not catch fish,  
No, sir, I do not catch fish,  
And I do not ferry people!”

1. Ivan walks along the Danube,  
Playing a pipe.  
2. – Ivan, are you fishing,  
Or are you ferrying people?  
3. – My crossings are bitter,  
Bitter tears have drowned me.  
4. We had a sister Pelahiya,  
She drowned on Sunday.  
5. She washed her feet on the stone,  
Spoke to the Danube:  
6. – You, Danube, calm water,  
Do not save me.  
7. Do not save me,  
If I am to drown.

1. Ivan walks over the Danube,  
Playing a pipe.  
2. – Ivan, are you fishing,  
Or are you ferrying people?  
3. – My crossings are bitter,  
Bitter tears have drowned me.  
4. We had a sister, Pelagia,  
She drowned on Sunday.  
5. She washed her feet on a stone,  
Spoke to the Danube:  
6. – You, Danube, quiet water,  
Do not save me.  
7. Do not save me  
If I am going to drown.

1. Ivan walks over the Danube,  
Playing the pipe.  
2. – Ivan, are you fishing,  
Or are you ferrying people?  
3. – My transports are bitter,  
Bitter tears have drowned me.  
4. We had a sister named Pelagia,  
She drowned on Sunday.  
5. She washed her feet on a stone,  
And spoke to the Danube:  
6. – You, Danube, quiet water,  
Do not let me be rescued.  
7. Do not let me be rescued,  
If I am to drown.

1. Oh, I am drunk – I am falling down,  
And I am afraid to go home.  
2. Because I have a wicked husband,  
He will beat me – I know well.  
3. He will beat me and also scold me,  
And drive me away from home.  
4. He will drive me from the house,  
Calling me a tramp.  
5. Oh, I will come to the Danube,  
I will stand and think to myself,  
6. Oh Danube-Danube,  
Accept me, your mistress:  
7. Accept my father, accept my mother,  
Accept me, a young woman.  
8. Accept all my family too,  
Just leave my husband...  
9. – Only now do I know grief,  
That I have four children.  
10. And a fifth wrapped in a swaddle,  
Drying my little head for me.  
11. Swim out, dear, from the Danube –  
Only now do I know grief.

1. A mother was supposed to have one daughter  
She bathed her in honey.  
2. Oh, she bathed and washed her  
But did not foresee happiness or fate.  
3. Did not foresee happiness or fate,  
She gave her in marriage to a drunkard.  
4. And the drunkard drinks and wanders,  
He goes home and scolds his wife:  
5. – Run, woman, drown yourself,  
Because you are no good to me.  
6. The woman went to the Danube,  
And spoke with the Danube:  
7. Oh Danube, little Danube,  
Take me as your mistress.  
8. You accepted my father, you accepted my mother,  
Accept me too, the young one.  
9. And you, pikes, eat my hands,  
And you, catfish, eat my legs.  
10. And water, wash my face,  
Come, dear, and look.  
11. The dear came to the Danube,  
And spoke with the Danube:  
12. Oh Danube, little Danube,  
Give me back my mistress.  
13. Because now I know trouble,  
Because I have five children.  
14. Four sons, one daughter,  
A little one in swaddling clothes.

1. I will sow sage  
Early Sunday morning.  
For some reason, the Lord is not favorable to me:  
The sage does not sprout.*  

2. And when it sprouts,  
It will wither from the wind,  
Oh my God, merciful God,  
A drunken man is coming.  

3. A drunken man is coming,  
I am afraid of him,  
Oh my God, merciful God,  
Where shall I hide?  

4. A small window,  
And narrow doors,  
Open up, moist earth, –  
Let me go under you.

2. And when it does sprout,

1. And I will sow sage  
Early on Sunday morning.  
And for some reason the Lord did not please me,  
The sage does not sprout.*  

2. Oh, even if it sprouts,  
It will wither from the wind.  
My dear is coming from the tavern, from the celebration,  
He does not look at me.  

3. Oh, even if he looks,  
I am afraid of him,  
Poor me, poor little head,  
Where shall I go?  

4. The windows are small,  
And the door is narrow.  
– Part for me, damp earth,  
For me, a young girl.  

5. And the earth parted,  
My dear breathed out.  
– Oh my God, my God,  
What have I sinned?

1. And I will sow sage  
Early on Sunday morning,  
For some reason my little God won’t grow,  
Sage... the sage will not sprout.*  
2. Even if it does sprout,  
It will wither from the sun,  
My beloved will come from the tavern drunk,  
He will not look at me... at me.  
3. Even if he looks,  
I am afraid of him,  
Poor little head of mine,  
Where shall I... where shall I hide?  
4. The windows are small,  
And the doors narrow...  
— Part, damp earth,  
Let me... let me go under you!  
5. The earth parted,  
My dear stepped back:  
— Farewell, farewell, damp earth,  
What have I... what have I sinned?

1. You gave me away, mother,  
Beyond the high mountains,  
You did not give me wine,  
Only hungry bees.  

2. The bees flew away,  
And I was left behind,  
Wicked fate  
Dried up my head.  

3. You gave me away, mother,  
To a foreign land,  
Like a little hemp plant  
Trampled in the swamp.  

4. Like those little hemp plants  
That rot in the swamp,  
Oh, how miserable it is for me  
To live in a foreign land.  

5. – Let me go, my dear,  
To the ford for water,  
To the ford for water, –  
To visit my kin.  

6. – I myself will not treat you  
And will not let you go,  
Because you will tell the kin  
About our disagreement.  

7. – Even if they ask, –  
I will not tell.  
Oh, whether good or bad, –  
I must get used to it.  

8. Even if they ask, –  
I will not tell,  
Tears are flowing,  
And I will leave the house.

1. You gave me away, mother,  
Beyond the high mountains,  
You did not give me wine,  
Only the hungry bees.  

2. The bees flew away,  
And I was left behind,

Dried up my head.

4. Like that hemp
