1. My mother gave me away,
Far away from herself,
Now she walks and looks out,
To see if I come to her.

2. My mother gave me away,
Beyond the high mountains,
But she did not give me wine,
Only fierce bees.

3. And those bees went to the field,
And I stayed behind,
So that a cruel fate
Would wither my head.

4. The daughter came to her father
And stood in the corner.
Her father asks:
– Why have you come, daughter?

5. – I have come, come, my father,
For that wine,
Oh, because of that wine
I have not eaten for three days.

6. The father gives four oxen,
The mother – two cows:
– Drive, daughter, this wine,
Foresee the cruel fate.

7. When she drove them to the gate
And started to shout:
– Come out, come out, cruel fate,
Help me drive them in!

8. But those strong-horned oxen
Only nudge with their horns,
Yet already carry my only one
Out to the wind.

9. But those strong-horned oxen
Only push with their sides,
Yet already carry my only one
To the cemetery.

1. And my mother gave me  
Beyond the high mountains,  
But she did not give me wine,  
Only the bees.  

2. I, the little bee, very early  
Flew out into the field,  
Could it be that my ill fate  
Turned my head?  

3. And my mother gave me  
Beyond the forest, beyond the reeds,  
And told me to come  
After a year for a visit.  

4. The daughter did not listen to her mother,  
She came at the moon,  
The mother did not recognize her daughter,  
She asked the neighbor.  

5. – Neighbor, dear little dove,  
Is this your daughter?  
A blouse embroidered  
With bloody flowers.  

6. The daughter came to her father  
And stood in the corner...  
The father looks, his heart withers:  
– Why have you come, daughter?  

7. – And you, father, do not ask,  
You do not tell me,  
And come on, father, give me four oxen,  
Because I am being driven out of the house.  

8. Oh, the father gave four oxen,  
The mother – two cows:  
– Drive, drive, dear daughter,  
And endure your ill fate.  

9. And she drove them to the gate  
And began to shout:  
– Come out, come out, ill fate,  
Help to drive them in!  

10. And ill fate did not come out,  
His mother came out:  
– You lie, you lie, only one,  
This is not your mother.  

11. I see those gray oxen  
Wandering in the yard,  
I see our newlyweds  
Fighting in the house.  

12. I see those gray oxen  
Clashing with their horns,  
I see our only one  
Being carried out into the wind.  

13. I see those gray oxen  
Do not have horns,  
I see our only one  
Washed on the bench.  

14. And there were good neighbors,  
They let the old mother know:  
– Go, go, old mother,  
To bury your daughter.  

15. The father runs, the mother runs,  
The whole family runs:  
– Open up, sorrowful doors,  
Here is our child.  

16. – My daughter, my only one,  
And what have you done wrong,  
And why has your red blood  
Stained the ground?

Filename: Kmeln_ballads_587_Oi pidu ya v lis po drova
translation:
1. Oh, I will go to the forest for firewood  
And will break off a stick.  
Foolish thought led me  
To a foreign land.  

2. And in the foreign land  
There is neither father nor mother.  
My head aches –  
There is nothing to bind it with.  

3. Oh, I will bind my little head  
With a silk handkerchief,  
I will send it to my family  
By a gray falcon.  

4. But the silk handkerchief  
Will not bind the head.  
The gray falcon does not speak –  
It will not tell the truth.

1. Oh, beyond the mountain  
Beyond the limestone,  
My dear does not live  
Truly with me.*  
2. I prepare for him  
A paper bed,  
He prepares for me  
A wire whip.  
3. The paper bed  
Flew away with the wind,  
The wire whip  
Boiled with blood.  
4. – Let me go, my dear,  
To walk in the cherry orchard,  
I swear, I will not  
Pick any berries.  
5. I will pick a rose flower  
And set it afloat on the water:  
– Float, rose flower,  
All the way to my kin.  
6. The rose flower floated,  
Reached the Danube,  
Mother took water  
And recognized the flower.  
7. – My daughter, my daughter,  
Were you sick,  
That your flower  
Withered on the water?  
8. – Mother, you gave me  
Beyond the high mountains,  
Now you look out,  
Like the bright dawn.  
9. Mother, you gave me  
Beyond the forest, beyond the oak,  
Now you look out,  
Wondering if I’m coming to visit?  
10. Sister in the yard, –  
Brother is chopping wood,  
Brother is chopping wood,  
Asks the sister:  
11. – I did not marry off my son,  
Did not give away my daughters,  
Why then, my sister,  
Have you become so old?  
12. – I did not marry off my son,  
Because I was late;  
Did not give away my daughters,  
Because I did not rise early.  
13. Oh, daughter, come inside, –  
Mother is baking bread.  
Mother is baking bread,  
Asks her daughter:  
14. – I did not marry off my son,  
Did not give away my daughters,  
Why then, my daughter,  
Have you become so old?  
15. – I did not marry off my son,  
Because I was late;  
Did not give away my daughters,  
Because I did not rise early.  
16. But you do not ask,  
Do not tell me:  
Oh, whether good or bad, –  
I must get used to it.

Filename: Kmeln_ballads_590_Oi pidu ya v lis po drova
translation:
1. Oh, I will go to the forest to gather firewood  
And break off some branches,  
Foolish reason has led me  
To a foreign land.  

2. In a foreign land  
There is neither father nor mother,  
Oh, my little head aches –  
There is no one to tell.  

3. Oh, I am all alone, completely alone,  
Like a blade of grass in the field,  
Oh, I will take a bucket  
And go for water.  

4. Oh, I came to the well  
But the crane broke down,  
People say that because of me  
The whole family has disowned me.  

5. Oh, I will pluck a flower from the rosebush  
And set it afloat on the water:  
– Float, float, little rose flower,  
All the way to my family.

Filename: Kmeln_ballads_591_Oi pidu ya v lis po drova
translation:
1. Oh, I will go to the forest for firewood  
And break some branches,  
Foolish mind led me  
To a strange... to a foreign land.*  
2. And in a foreign land  
There is no father, no mother,  
My head aches,  
There is nothing... nothing to bind it with.  
3. I will bind my head  
With a silk handkerchief,  
And send word to my family  
By a gray... gray falcon.  
4. But that silk handkerchief  
Will not bind my head,  
And that gray falcon  
Will not tell the truth... the real truth.  
5. Oh, I will tear a flower from the rose bush  
And set it afloat on the water:  
– Swim, swim, flower from the rose bush,  
All the way... all the way to my kin.  
6. The rose flower floated, floated  
And stopped at a ford,  
Mother came out to get water  
And recognized the flo... the flower.  
7. – Daughter, have you lain a year,  
Or been ill for two,  
That your rose flower  
Has withered on the wa... on the water?  
8. – No, mother, I have not lain a year,  
Nor been ill for two,  
I fell into a cruel fate, –  
Lost forever... forever gone.

Filename: Kmeln_ballads_591v2_Oi pidu ya v lis po drova
translation:
1. Oh, I will go to the forest for firewood  
And break some branches,  
Foolish thought led me  
To someone else's… to a foreign land.*

2. And in that foreign land  
There is no father, no mother,  
My little head ached,  
Nothing... nothing to bind it with.

3. I will bind my little head  
With a silk scarf,  
And send a message to my family  
With a gray... gray falcon.

4. But that silk scarf  
Will not bind my head,  
And that gray falcon  
Will not tell the truth... the truth.

5. Oh, I will pick a flower from the rose  
And send it down the water:  
— Float, float, little rose flower,  
All the way... all the way to my kin.

6. The rose flower floated and floated  
And stopped at the ford,  
My mother came out to fetch water  
And recognized the flo... the flower.

7. — Daughter, were you ill for a year,  
Or sick for two,  
That your rose flower  
Withered on the wa... on the water?

8. — No, mother, I was not ill for a year,  
Nor sick for two,  
I came upon a cruel fate —  
And was lost forever.

1. When my dear one went on a journey,  
My dear one went on a journey,  
2. He left me weak and unwell.  
3. When my dear one returned from the road  
4. He somehow soiled my white bed  
5. And frowned at me,  
A young woman.  
6. And in the evening  
The whip was clattering,  
7. At midnight  
The dear one was begging,  
8. And by morning  
The dear one breathed his last.  
9. The old mother  
Walks around the yard  
10. And gently speaks to her son:  
11. "Son, do you love your wife,  
12. That you do not wake her early?"  
13. "There is no one to wake anymore, mother,  
There is no one to love anymore, mother:  
14. She bled,  
I cannot approach her."  
15. "Saddle the black horse, son,  
16. And ride out from your yard."

1. There was a Hanya,  
Hanya Bondarivna,  
She had a mother-in-law  
Who was very unfaithful.  

2. And early on Sunday  
All the bells were ringing,  
But the mother-in-law sent Hanya  
To the field to reap:  

3. – Go, go, Hanya,  
And don’t look back,  
Reap the rye in the field  
And don’t bend over.  

4. Oh, Hanya ran  
And did not look back,  
She reaped, she reaped,  
And did not bend over.  

5. She ran home –  
A terrible moment,  
The little child  
Was left alone in the field.  

6. Run, Hanya, run  
Along that road,  
Where the falcon walks  
With a gray beard.  

7. – Falcon, falcon,  
You walk through the field,  
Have you seen the child  
Alone in the field?  

8. – Oh yes, I saw, I saw  
There, under the haystack,  
Oh, black crows  
Are tearing her hands.  

9. Oh, black crows  
Are tearing her hands,  
Little crow chicks  
Are pecking her eyes.

1. There was a Hanya,  
Hanya Bondarivna,  
She had a mother-in-law  
Who was very unfaithful.  

2. And early on Sunday  
All the bells were ringing,  
But the mother-in-law sent Hanya  
To the field to reap:  

3. – Go, go, Hanya,  
And do not look back,  
Reap the rye in the field  
And do not bend over.  

4. Oh, Hanya ran  
And did not look back,  
She reaped and reaped  
And did not bend over.  

5. She ran home –  
An unfortunate hour,  
The little child  
Was left alone in the field.  

6. Run, Hanya, run  
Along that road,  
Where the falcon walks  
With a gray beard.  

7. – Falcon, falcon,  
You walk through the field,  
Have you seen a little child  
In the field?  

8. – Oh yes, I saw, I saw  
Right there, under the stack,  
Oh, black crows  
Are tearing her by the hands.  

9. Oh, black crows  
Are tearing her by the hands,  
Little jackdaws  
Are pecking at her eyes.

1. And in the field, a birch tree,  
And in the field, a curly one,  
And on that birch tree  
A cuckoo was calling.*  
2. Oh, that is not a cuckoo,  
That is the dear mother.  
She was dressing  
Her son as a soldier:  
3. – Oh, come, my son,  
Don’t delay for long:  
In two years’ time  
Return home.  
4. Oh, one year passes,  
Another follows,  
The son comes home,  
And he has a wife.  
5. – Good evening, mother!  
Are you glad to see me?  
Are you glad to see me,  
Your daughter-in-law?  
6. – Oh, I am glad, son,  
I am glad to see you,  
But I am not glad, son,  
To see your wife.  
7. She seated her son  
At the corner of the table,  
The young daughter-in-law  
In the hallway by the door.  
8. And she poured him  
Sweet honey,  
And for the young daughter-in-law  
Green poison.  
9. They poured honey with poison  
Together,  
Like a dove and a dove,  
They bowed their heads.  
10. She buried her son  
In a green garden,  
The young daughter-in-law  
In a deep ravine.  
11. She planted red viburnum  
For her son,  
Thorny blackthorn  
For the young daughter-in-law.  
12. The red viburnum  
Withers and dries,  
The thorny blackthorn  
Blooms and prospers.

1. And in the field a birch tree,  
And in the field curly,  
And on that birch tree  
A cuckoo was calling.  

2. Oh, that is not a cuckoo,  
That is the dear mother.  
She was dressing up  
Her son as a soldier:  

3. – Oh, come, my son,  
Don’t delay long:  
In two years’ time  
Return home.  

4. Oh, one year passes,  
Another follows,  
The son is coming home,  
And has a wife.  

5. – Good evening, mother!  
Are you glad because of me?  
Are you glad because of me,  
Your daughter-in-law?  

6. – Oh, I am glad, son,  
I am glad because of you,  
But I am not glad, son,  
Because of your wife.  

7. She seated her son  
At the corner table,  
The young daughter-in-law  
In the hallway on the doorstep.  

8. And gave him  
Sweet honey,  
And to the young daughter-in-law  
Green poison.  

9. They poured the honey and poison  
Together,  
Like a dove and a female dove  
They bowed their heads.  

10. She buried her son  
In the green orchard,  
The young daughter-in-law  
In the deep ravine.  

11. She planted for her son  
Red viburnum,  
For the young daughter-in-law  
A thorny blackthorn.  

12. The red viburnum  
Withers, dries up,  
The thorny blackthorn  
Blooms, thrives.

1. And in the field, a birch tree,  
And in the field, a curly one,  
And on that birch tree  
A cuckoo was calling.

2. Oh, that is not a cuckoo,  
That is the dear mother.  
She was dressing up  
Her son to become a soldier;

3. – Oh, go, my son,  
Do not delay long:  
In two years’ time  
Return home.

4. Oh, one year passes,  
Another follows,  
The son comes home,  
And he has a wife.

5. – Good evening, mother!  
Are you glad to see me?  
Are you glad of me,  
Your wife?

6. – Oh, I am glad, son,  
I am glad of you,  
But I am not glad, son,  
Of your wife.

7. She seated the son  
At the corner of the table,  
The young daughter-in-law  
In the entry hall on the doorstep.

8. And she poured for him  
Sweet honey,  
For the young daughter-in-law  
Green poison.

9. They drank the honey and poison  
Together,  
Like a dove and a dove mate  
They bowed their heads.

10. She buried the son  
In the green little garden,  
The young daughter-in-law  
In a deep ravine.

11. She planted for the son  
Red viburnum,  
For the young daughter-in-law  
Prickly thornbush.

12. The red viburnum  
Withers, dries up,  
The prickly thornbush  
Blooms, prospers.

1. – Are you glad, mother,  
That there are two of us coming?  
2. That there are two of us  
Coming into your yard?  
3. – I am glad for my son,  
Because he is my child,  
4. But I am not glad for the daughter-in-law –  
I have never loved her.  
5. I seated my son  
At the carved table,  
6. And the young daughter-in-law  
By the hay threshold.  
7. I gave my son  
Sweet honey to drink,  
8. And to the young daughter-in-law  
I gave Gadyach poison.  
9. The daughter-in-law did not drink it,  
She left it for the dear one,  
10. And my son drank it –  
Clutched his heart.  
11. – Oh my God, my God,  
What have I done:  
12. That I lost  
Two children together!  
13. I buried my son  
In the church behind the door,  
14. The young daughter-in-law  
Among the fences.  
15. I planted a green maple  
Over my son,  
16. And a white birch  
Over the young daughter-in-law.  
17. The maple leans  
Over the birch,  
18. And my old mother's  
Heart breaks.

1. – Are you glad, mother,  
That there are two of us going?  
2. – I am glad for my son,  
The son is my child,  
3. I am not glad for my daughter-in-law, –  
Because I did not love her.  
4. I poured green beer for my son,  
5. For my daughter-in-law I poured poison with spells.  
6. The son did not drink the wine, –  
He poured it under the horse,  
7. He got afflicted by the spells and the poison,  
8. And with the black horse  
He bent down wearily.  
9. – Oh my God, my God,  
What have I done,  
10. That I lost two children  
From the world.  
11. – If you knew, mother,  
How to enchant us,  
12. Then know, mother,  
How to bury us.  
13. She buried the son  
In the church behind the door,  
14. And the poor daughter-in-law  
In the church on the threshold.  
15. On the son grew  
A green maple,  
16. On the poor daughter-in-law –  
A white birch.  
17. The breeze blows, blows,  
The breeze softly blows,  
18. [The maple] leans  
Toward the birch,  
19. Leaf to leaf  
They close together.

1. Oh, behind the green grove  
A widow was picking fine flax.  
2. She picked, picked, and selected,  
Sang plaintively.  
3. – Allow me, mother, to marry,  
Allow me, mother, to take the widow.

1. Oh, beyond the green grove  
A widow was picking fine flax.  
2. She picked, picked, and selected,  
And sang a mournful song.  
3. – Allow me, mother, to marry,  
Allow me, mother, to take the widow.

1. Oh, behind the green grove  
A widow was picking fine flax.  
2. She picked, she picked, she chose,  
And sang plaintively.  
3. – Allow me, mother, to marry,  
Allow me, mother, to take the widow.

1. Oh, by the green grove*  
A widow was picking fine flax.  
2. She was picking, choosing,  
Giving a thin voice.  
3. There Vasylko is mowing grass,  
Carrying the thin voice.  
4. He threw the scythe into the grass,  
And went back home.  
5. And he went back home  
And sat down on a bench.  
6. And he sat down on a bench,  
And bowed his head.  
7. – Why are you sitting and thinking?  
Why don’t you drink or have fun?  
8. – Allow me to marry the widow,  
I will drink and have fun even more.  
9. – I will not allow you to marry the widow,  
The widow knows how to cast spells.  
10. She charmed her husband,  
And will charm my son as well.

1. Oh, from behind the mountain,  
From behind the tall one,  
A strong wind  
Is blowing...  
2. A strong wind  
Is blowing,  
And the father  
Is sending off his son:  
3. – Oh, go, my son,  
Far away from me,  
Because your wife  
Is disagreeable:  
4. Neither sincerity,  
Nor joy,  
Neither dark brows,  
Nor kind words.  
5. I took my dear beloved  
By the white hand  
And led her  
To the Danube River,  
6. And bought her  
A new ship, –  
A new ship  
And even a saddle...  
7. A new ship  
And even a saddle:  
– So sit down, sit down,  
My darling!  
8. The ship sails,  
As the Danube roars,  
And the child cries, –  
As the meadows rustle.  
9. And the child cries, –  
As the meadows rustle,  
And my beloved sits,  
Like a candle burning.  
10. The ship sailed out  
Under thick vines,  
And delicate tears  
Streamed down my beloved.  
11. I came, my dear,  
And home,  
And bowed, bowed  
Her little head on the table:  
12. Neither sincerity,  
Nor joy,  
Neither dark brows,  
Nor kind words!

1. Yes, a dark cloud came,  
The mother did not love her daughter.  
2. But the mother did not love her daughter,  
Winter touched her.  
3. And the father did not look at his son:  
– Oh, go, son, wherever you want.  
4. And the son went out beyond the gate  
And stood there like an orphan.  
5. And the elder sister leads the horse,  
The middle one carries the saddle.  
6. And I (the youngest, the smallest)  
Open the narrow little gate  
And ask my brother:  
7. – Oh, brother, Vasyl,  
When will you be visiting?  
8. And take, sister, a handful of rye,  
Scatter it over the stone.  
9. And when the stone grows rye,  
Then the brother will come as a guest!

1. The fog rolled down the ravine,  
The father quarreled with his son:  
2. "Go away from me, son,  
Because of you I have sorrow."  
3. The son slips away from his father,  
Washing his face with tears.  
4. Washing his face with tears,  
He says goodbye to his sisters.  
5. The eldest sister leads the horse,  
And the second carries the saddle.  
6. The youngest is sewing a scarf,  
She washes it with her tears.  
7. "Oh, brother, oh our brother,  
When will you come to visit us?"  
8. "Take, sister, a handful of sand,  
Sow it on the stone."  
9. "When that sand grows like rye,  
Then your brother will come to visit."  
10. There is no rye—no ear of grain,  
No brother—no voice.  
11. There is no rye—the water washed it away,  
No brother—the bullet killed him.

1. (And) The grave in the field
Spoke to the wind:
– Blow, stormy wind,
So that I do not blacken.*
2. So that I do not blacken,
So that I do not wither,
So that grass grows on me,
Grows, greens.
3. And the sun does not warm,
And the wind does not blow,
In the steppe by the roadside
The grave darkens.

1. Oh, Vasilechko drowned,  
Drowned, drowned,  
Bought himself a new boat,  
So he wouldn’t drown.*  
2. Oh, a fierce wind blew  
From the deep ravine: –  
It overturned Vasilechko  
In the middle of the Danube!  
3. The mother walks, her arms ache  
From the little finger:  
There is no, there is no and there will not be  
Vasyl’s beloved!  
4. – Oh you, fishermen boys,  
Here are four coins for you,  
Just find Vasilechko, –  
He is handsome, good!  
5. They spotted Vasilechko  
For thirty-two hours,  
And they found Vasilechko  
In the deep valley.  
6. They carve the maple, they carve the maple,  
They carve the maple tree, –  
For young Vasyl  
They make an oak coffin**.  
7. They carry Vasyl, lead the horse,  
The horse stumbles;  
Behind Vasyl, his own mother  
Faints from grief.  
8. They carry Vasyl, lead the horse,  
The horse bows its head, –  
Behind Vasyl, his own mother  
Breaks her white hands.  
9. She breaks her white hands  
From the little finger:  
There is no, there is no and there will not be  
Vasyl’s beloved!

1. After Vasyl, after Vasyl,  
Where has Vasyl gone?  
If Vasyl were mowing in the grove,  
The grove would be green.  

2. If Vasyl were mowing in the grove, –  
The birds would be chirping;  
If Vasyl were lying in the grove, –  
Musicians would be playing.  

3. After Vasyl, after Vasyl,  
Where has Vasyl gone?  
He bought himself a new boat,  
So he wouldn't drown.  

4. Strong winds blew  
From the green grove,  
They capsized little Vasyl  
In the middle of the Danube.  

5. – Oh, you fishermen boys,  
I’ll give four coins each,  
Save little Vasyl,  
For he is very good.  

6. They lead the horse, carry the body,  
The horse lowers its head,  
For Vasyl his mother  
Breaks her white hands.

1. Why did I become a widow,  
My house burned down.  
2. – My brothers, falcons,  
Accept me as a neighbor.  
3. – Our sister, little quail,  
Why do you have so many children?  
4. When we sit down to eat,  
The children will be peeking in.  
5. – When you sit down to eat,  
I will send the children out of the house:  
6. – Go away, children, out of the house,  
The men will be having dinner.  
7. While the men were dining,  
The children visited their father:  
8. – Oh, daddy, our falcon,  
Take us with you.  
9. – Oh, I’m glad to take you,  
But I cannot get up from here!  
10. The damp earth covered me,  
Closed my black eyes.

1. Oh, do not bend, dear pear tree,  
Do not grieve, beautiful girl.  
2. Do not grieve, and you will give yourself away,  
And satisfy your own heart.  
3. Under the white birch tree  
Brother sits with his sister.  
4. Brother sits, playing the pipe,  
Sister sits, shedding tears.  
5. – Oh, why are you crying, sister,  
Do you want payment for your work?  
6. – Oh, I do not want payment, brother,  
For we had only one mother.  
7. Oh, we had only one mother,  
She was not unfair.  
8. To you she gave oxen and a field,  
To me she gave two sticks.  
9. She gave me two sticks  
For unfortunate chores.  
10. For me to sew and embroider,  
To feed small children,  
11. To reap and gather with a sickle,  
To endure a hard fate.  
12. – Come, sister, on Sunday,  
I will share half of the estate.  
13. I, the sister, listened,  
I went on Sunday.  
14. I, the sister, am still on the road,  
Her brother is at the doorstep.  
15. And the sister is arriving,  
Brother locks the house:  
16. – Hide, woman, the bread and salt from the table,  
Because my sister is coming.  
17. Hide, woman, the bread and salt in the chest,  
Because I am coming to visit.  
18. – Oh, stop, brother, do not be afraid,  
Do not hide from bread and salt.  
19. I did not come to dine,  
I only came to visit.  
20. Not you, but your children,  
Should know their aunt.  
21. They should know, remember,  
So that there would be no matchmaking within the family.  
22. I have a house on the veranda,  
Come, brother, to visit us.  
23. Decorated with guelder-rose,  
Come, brother, with your wife.  
24. Decorated with flowers,  
Come, brother, with the children.

1. Under the white birch  
A little brother sits with his sister.  
2. The little brother sits – writing letters,  
The sister sits – embroidering.  
3. The sister sits – embroidering,  
Drenching with tears.  
4. "Oh, why are you crying, sister,  
Perhaps you want payment from me?"  
5. "I do not want payment, brother,  
We were born of the same mother.  
6. We were born of the same mother,

1. A small river flows

Has no sense,

In reception.

6. To whom, comrade,  
Should I leave this girl?  
7. – Oh, to me, comrade,  
To me, to me,  
8. I will take care  
Even better of you.  
9. – Oh, take care, comrade,  
Of a good girl,  
10. When I return from the soldiers, –  
We will drink some vodka.  
11. Oh, I came from the soldiers  
To a new house,

Learning to crawl.

Take care of you,  
14. That already my girl  
Has a little child.  
15. – Not big, comrade,  
Not a great sorrow,

It will be a help!

1. I was out in the field, working hard,  
Coming home with a hoe,  
I enter the tavern,  
My beloved is drinking with my godmother.  
2. – Come on, woman, have a shot  
And then go home,  
And cook dinner,  
I will come with the godmother.  
3. Not early, not late,  
The moon has not yet risen,  
But already my beloved with the godmother  
Is coming home.  
4. – Oh, godmother, my dear,  
Prepare food for me,  
I am about to sit down  
To have dinner with your beloved.  
5. – May it never happen, my godmother,  
That I would endure this,  
That I would give you a place to stay  
In my own house.  
6. Sit down, my beloved, dark-browed,  
On the white woolen cloth,  
And you, godmother, sit in the house  
Like a stone on my heart.  
7. – I am sitting, godfather, I am sitting, my love,  
And I will keep sitting,  
And you will come out of the house  
And take the children away.

1. And in the black poplar tree of the town  
It bowed down low,  
In someone else's village  
With the second wife  
My beloved got married.*  
2. – Harness the horse, brother,  
The gray-maned horse,  
And let's go  
To a foreign village  
To visit my beloved.

It was not cheerful there,  
When we came  
To the yard,  
My beloved had a wedding.  
4. And when we entered the house,  
We didn’t know what to say, –  
Whether to greet,  
Or to ask questions,  
Or to turn back.  
5. And the girl Tetyana  
Stood on the threshold,  
Leaning slightly  
On the doorway,  
Saddened deeply.

Who has come to us:  
Oh, is it a stranger,  
Oh, is it family,  
Or is it your first wife?  
7. – Oh, girl Tetyana,  
This has come to us:  
It is neither a stranger,  
Nor family,  
It is my first wife.  
8. – Man Mykhailo,  
What a deceiver you are,  
And you swore  
And pledged,  
That you were not yet married.  
9. – Oh, girl Tetyana,  
That will not be bad, –  
Comb your braid,  
Braid the ribbon,  
Go as you used to go.

1. In our village  
There happened news:  
Kateryna deceived  
The widow's son.  

2. – Leave the woman, Ivan,  
I will leave the children  
And we will go to Ukraine,  
There we will live.  

3. – I am afraid, Kateryna,  
That they might catch us,  
And that our white hands  
Will be tied back.  

4. – Don’t be afraid, Kateryna,  
No one can catch us,  
I even sent my husband  
To the field to plow.  

5. Semen is plowing, plowing  
With the old grandfather,  
Kateryna looks out  
With an early lunch.  

6. Semen is plowing, plowing,  
Looking at the road,  
All the women bring lunch,  
But mine does not.  

7. Semen finished plowing  
The green forest,  
Unharnesses the gray oxen  
And goes home.  

8. When he came home,  
To the new house,  
He asks the dear children:  
– Where is our mother?  

9. – Our mother went  
To the forest for viburnum,  
And as she left, she said  
That she would leave you.  

10. Our mother went  
To the forest for calves,  
And as she left, she said:  
– My little orphans.  

11. Semen looked  
Into the new chest:  
No scarves, no skirt,  
No mistress.  

12. When Semen struck  
The floor with his hands:  
– My dear children,  
I am lost with you.  

13. Strike, frosts,  
On the dry vines,  
Freeze Kateryna  
For the orphans’ tears.

1. Beyond the mountains, high mountains,  
Beyond the mountains, the sun is burning,  
Far, far away is my beloved,  
I remember, and my heart aches.  

2. Once on a Sunday morning,  
As the nightingale sang a song,  
I quietly entered the room,  
As my father-in-law was reading a note.  
Then he recited the text:  

3. "Come, come, daughter-in-law, to the table,  
Listen to what my dear wrote,  
He writes that he loves another,  
He wrote to you: forget him."  

4. I said nothing to that,  
But kept saying only one thing:  
"I would have already forgotten him long ago,  
But it’s a pity, because the child is his."  

5. I took four packs of matches  
And dissolved them in warm water,  
At four o’clock at night  
I took poison for my heart.  

6. Four weeks have already passed,  
Since my father-in-law buried his daughter-in-law,  
And that beloved writes a note:  
"I was only joking."  

7. Oh father, oh father, oh father,  
Why did you tell her that?  
I never married the other one,  
I was just testing her character.

1. Oh, in the deep ravine  
Grows a tall blackthorn, yes  
Hey, hey, oh lullaby,  
Grows a tall blackthorn.*  
2. And from under that blackthorn  
A widow came out.  
3. The widow came out,  
Carrying out two sons.  
4. Carrying out two sons –  
Ivanyush and Vasyl.  
5. Ivanyush and Vasyl  
She also swaddled in silk.  
6. She also swaddled them in silk  
And sent them to the Danube:  
7. – Oh you, Danube, you blue one,  
Take my two sons.  
8. And you, pike and tench,  
Feed my two sons.  
9. Feed my two sons –  
Ivanyush and Vasyl.  
10. About twenty-five years later  
The widow went to get water.  
11. She began to draw water,  
The water began to rise.  
12. The water began to rise,  
A ship started to arrive.  
13. And on that ship  
There stand two young men.  
14. One stands on watch,  
Combing his hair.  
15. The other stands on the Don  
And winks at the widow:  
16. – Oh you, young widow,  
Will you go with me?  
17. – What kind of world is this now,  
That the son does not recognize his mother?  
18. Let's go, son, to the monastery,  
May God forgive us.  
19. Let's go, son, into the dark forest,  
Let the beast eat us!

1. Oh, in a deep ravine  
Grows a tall blackthorn, yes  
Hey, hey, oh lullaby,  
A tall blackthorn grows.  
2. And from under that blackthorn  
A widow came out.  
3. The widow came out,  
Carrying two sons.  
4. Carrying two sons –  
Ivanyushka and Vasyl.  
5. Ivanyushka and Vasyl  
She also wrapped in silk.  
6. She wrapped them in silk  
And let them go onto the Danube:  
7. – Oh you, Danube, you blue one,  
Take my two sons.  
8. And you, pike, and also tench,  
Feed my two sons.  
9. Feed my two sons –  
Ivanyushka and Vasyl.  
10. About twenty-five years later  
The widow went to fetch water.  
11. She stood to fetch water,  
The water began to rise.  
12. The water began to rise,  
A ship began to approach.  
13. And on that ship  
There stand two young men.  
14. One stands on watch,  
Combing his hair.  
15. The other stands at the helm  
And winks at the widow:  
16. – Oh you, young widow,  
Will you go with me?  
17. – What kind of world has come now,  
That a mother’s son does not recognize her?  
18. Let’s go, son, to the monastery,  
May God forgive us.  
19. Let’s go, son, to the dark forest,  
May the wild beast devour us!

1. Oh, in the deep ravine  
Grows a high blackthorn,  
Hey (hey), hu-ha-ha,  
Grows a high blackthorn.*  
2. And from under that blackthorn  
Came out a widow.  
3. The widow came out,  
Carrying two sons.  
4. Carried two sons:  
Ivanyusha and Vasyl.  
5. Ivanyusha, Vasyl  
And threw them into the sea:  
6. – You, yellow sand,  
Feed my little children;  
7. You, wide water,  
Give the children milk.  
8. The widow made a vow  
That she would not go to the sea.  
9. In the twenty-fifth year  
The widow came for water.  
10. The widow began to draw water,  
The ship came sailing up.  
11. – Oh, you, widow-widow,  
Will you go with me?  
12. – For Ivan, I go myself,  
For Vasyl, I send my daughter.  
13. The widow began to marry,  
The church began to creak.  
14. This is the world we live in now –  
A brother did not recognize his sister.  
15. Now the time is such  
That a mother goes after her son.

1. Oh, in the deep ravine  
Grows a tall blackthorn,  
Hey (hey), (hey), (yo-ha-ha),  
Grows a tall blackthorn.*  
2. And from under that blackthorn  
A widow came out.  
3. The widow came out,  
Carrying two sons.  
4. She carried two sons:  
Ivanyusha and Vasyl.  
5. Ivanyusha, Vasyl  
And threw them into the sea:  
6. – You yellow sand,  
Feed my little children;  
7. You, wide meadow,  
Give milk to the children.  
8. The widow swore  
That she would not go to the sea.  
9. In the twenty-fifth hour  
The widow came for water.  
10. The widow began to draw water,  
A ship came sailing up.  
11. – Oh, you widow-widow,  
Will you come with me?  
12. – For Ivan I will go myself,  
For Vasyl I will send my daughter.  
13. The widow began to marry,  
The church began to creak.  
14. This is the kind of world we live in now –  
The brother did not recognize the sister.  
15. Now such a time has come  
That a mother goes after her son.

1. A widow lived in Podil,  
Oh, yo, yo, yo, yo!  
God did not grant her freedom,  
Oh, my sorrow.*  
2. Only He gave her three sons,  
Three sons and three daughters.  
3. And the eldest got married,  
The middle one was devoured by beasts.  
4. And the youngest got lost,  
Wandered into the open field.  
5. In the open field a fire burns,  
A fire burns, a robber sits.  
6. The robber sits, cooking porridge,  
Cooking porridge, luring the girl.  
7. – Stay, girl, with our faith,  
You will eat porridge with us.  
8. Eat porridge, spread the bed,  
Spread the bed – lie down to sleep.

1. Oh, a widow lived  
At the edge of the village,  
2. The widow had  
As many as three sons.  
3. As many as three sons  
And three daughters.  
4. The sons grew up –  
They became robbers.  
5. The daughters grew up –  
They got married.  
6. One daughter  
Drowned herself,  
7. And the second  
Hanged herself,  
8. And the third  
Herded bulls.  
9. Oh, in the grove  
A fire is burning,  
10. By the fire  
Robbers are sitting.  
11. One says:  
– Let's kill him.  
12. The second says:  
– Let's ask first.

1. Oh, beyond the grove, the grove,  
By the edge of a clear field,  
There lived a poor widow  
In quiet Podil.*  
2. A poor widow lived  
In quiet Podil,  
But that widow had neither  
Happiness nor fate.  
3. The poor widow only had  
Nine sons in total,  
And a tenth, Halya,  
Beautiful in appearance.  
4. The sons grew up,  
Became robbers,  
And the beautiful Halya  
Was given to a merchant.  
5. Oh, the merchant went  
To trade goods,  
He took Halya with him  
To count the money.  
6. The evil robbers  
Killed the merchant,  
And took the beautiful Halya  
With them.  
7. They divided the money,  
Lay down to sleep,  
And the oldest robber  
Began to question Halya:  
8. – Oh Halya, you, Halya,  
Beautiful in appearance,  
Tell me the truth,  
What family are you from?  
9. – My mother had  
Nine sons in total,  
And I am their sister,  
Beautiful in appearance.  
10. – Get up, brothers,  
What have we done?  
We have separated our own sister  
From her mate.

1. In Kyiv, in Podil,  
A news happened:  
Philip loved Yarinochka,  
The family did not know.  

2. Oh, as they grew up,  
They began to love each other,  
But the father and mother  
Did not allow them to marry.  

3. The father and mother did not allow,  
Nor the sisters and brothers,  
Because Yarinochka was poor,  
And Philip was rich.  

4. – Philip, my son,  
Do not take Yaryna,  
Look after yourself, my son,  
Within your own ranks:  

5. With cows and oxen,  
Rich, with money,  
But do not take Yarinochka,  
Who shows her shoulders.  

6. – Oh, even though she is poor,  
She is dear to my heart, –  
If only she strives  
And also knows how to work.  

7. Oh, on the hill, on Golgotha,  
There the hill is steep,  
Oh, there runs a swift river,  
Swift, deep.  

8. There Philip and Yaryna  
Walked and talked,  
Heart clasped with heart,  
Hands froze.  

9. Heart clasped with heart,  
Hands froze,  
Oh, there Philip and Yaryna  
Drowned at the bottom.  

10. Let the fathers with mothers  
Know this misfortune:  
Where two children love each other,  
Let them not be parted.  

11. Where two children love each other,  
Let them not be parted,  
Let them, the young ones,  
Be united in marriage.

1. In Kyiv, in Podil  
A news occurred:  
Pylyp loved Yaryna,  
But the family did not know.  

2. Oh, as they grew up,  
They started to love each other,  
But the father and mother did not allow  
Them to marry.  

3. The father and mother did not allow it,  
Nor the sisters and brothers,  
Because Yaryna was poor,  
And Pylyp was rich.  

4. – Pylypone, my son,  
Do not take Yaryna,  
Look for yourself, my son,  
Among your equals:  

5. Among cows and oxen,  
The rich, with money,  
But do not take little Yaryna,  
Who shines with her shoulders.  

6. – Oh, though she is poor,  
She is dear to my heart, –  
If only she would try  
And know how to work.  

7. Oh, on the hill, on Golgotha,  
There is a steep hill,  
Oh, there runs a swift river,  
Swift, deep.  

8. There, Pylyp and Yaryna went and talked,  
Heart to heart they joined,  
Their hands froze.  

9. Heart to heart they joined,  
Their hands froze,  
Oh, there Pylyp and Yaryna  
Sank to the bottom.  

10. Let the fathers and mothers  
Know this misfortune:  
Where two children love each other,  
Let them not be separated.  

11. Where two children love each other,  
Let them not be separated,  
Let them, young ones,  
Be joined in marriage.

1. Oh, in the field at the boundary  
A piece of news happened:  
Philip loved Marynochka,  
The family did not know.  
2. Oh, as they grew up,  
They began to love each other,  
But the father and mother did not allow  
Them to marry.  
3. The father and mother did not allow,  
Nor the sister, nor the brothers,  
Because Maryna was poor,  
And Philip was rich.  
4. – Oh, here, my son,  
Take a braid and a rose,  
Whichever you want, take it  
As your wife.  
5. Oh, if poor, then with oxen,  
If rich, then with money,  
Just do not take a ragged one,  
Because she shows her shoulders.  
6. – A ragged one is a lover,  
Dear to my heart, –  
If she tried  
And also loved me.  
7. Oh, early on Sunday morning  
All the bells ring,  
Marynochka and Philip  
Are carried out from the pond.

1. Oh, the moon came out from behind the cloud  
And illuminated the whole valley,  
And when it lit up the whole valley,  
It rolled back behind the cloud again.*  

2. And in that valley stands a house,  
And in that house a fire is burning,  
There a mother is teaching her daughter,  
The daughter sits in tears.  

3. – Oh daughter, daughter, my daughter,  
You are my only dear one,  
Many girls have gotten married,  
But why do you sit alone?  

4. – Don’t give me to an old man,  
An old man is not a match for me,  
If you give me to him,  
I will rot in the damp earth.  

5. And nothing more was heard,  
Only the daughter’s crying,  
On the second hand, without love,  
Halya was attaching towels.  

6. She attached, she cried, she sobbed,  
Remembered the oath she swore,  
To love her beloved until death,  
But that oath was not fulfilled.  

7. Halya ran all the way to the sea  
And threw herself into the water,  
And suddenly in the middle of the field  
The neighing of a horse was heard.  

8. Oh, the moon came out from behind the cloud  
And illuminated the whole valley:  
Two corpses were floating on the sea,  
And the horse was walking along the shore.

1. Oh, the moon came out from behind the cloud  
And lit up the whole valley,  
And as it lit up the whole valley,  
It rolled back behind the cloud again.*  
2. And in that valley stands a house,  
And in that house a fire burns,  
There a mother is teaching her daughter,  
The daughter sits in tears.  
3. – Oh daughter, daughter, my daughter,  
You are my only treasure,  
Many girls have gotten married,  
But why do you sit alone?  
4. – Don't give me to an old man,  
An old man is no match for me,  
If you give me to him,  
I will rot in the damp earth.  
5. And nothing more was heard,  
Only the daughter's crying,  
And on the other hand, without love,  
Halya was hanging out the towels.  
6. She hung them, cried, sobbed,  
Remembered the vow she had sworn,  
To love her beloved until death,  
But that vow was not kept.  
7. Halya ran all the way to the sea  
And threw herself into the water,  
And suddenly, in the middle of the field,  
The neighing of a horse was heard.  
8. Oh, the moon came out from behind the cloud  
And lit up the whole valley:  
Two corpses were floating on the sea,  
And the horse was walking along the shore.

1. Come in, come in, little moon,  
Come over the mountain,  
Let me stand with my beloved  
This evening.  
2. – Oh girl, little girl,  
I have something to say:  
That neither father nor mother  
Wants to take you, poor one.  
3. – If neither father nor mother  
Wants to take me, poor one,  
Then go to the edge of the world  
To look for rich girls.  
4. When you get married,  
I will not be upset,  
I will come and stand at the wedding,  
Watching from the side.  
5. I will look at you,  
And you at me, –  
Small tears will fall  
From you and from me.  
6. When I come home, –  
I will die of grief,  
And you, my beloved dark-browed one,  
Will build a coffin.  
7. And very early on Sunday,  
All the bells will ring,  
And that girl  
Will be taken to the grave.  
8. Father cries, mother cries,  
The whole family cries,  
And my beloved, dark-browed one  
Has turned black as the earth.  
9. His father and mother stand,  
Smiling,  
Because their young son  
Is parting.  
10. – May it be granted to you, father and mother,  
You have not yet known good,  
That you have parted me, young,  
From my match.

Filename: Vinnytsia_ballads_579_Oj u luzi, v luzi kalyna
translation:
1. Oh, in the meadow, in the meadow, the viburnum,
Oh, in the meadow, in the meadow, it bent down.
2. Oh, in the meadow, in the meadow, it bent down,
A young girl fell in love.
3. The young girl, hey, fell in love,
She went into the blue sea and drowned.
4. She went into the blue sea and drowned,
And her mother grieved for her.
5. – Oh, why did you drown yourself, daughter,
Oh, who did you fall in love with, daughter?
6. When the Cossack arrived, hey, from Ukraine
And tied his horse to the viburnum.
7. – Red viburnum, bend down,
And young girl, call out.
8. – My dear Cossack, hey, I will stay here,
Whom I truly love, I will not forget.

1. Oh willow, willow,  
Green willow,  
Why are your leaves falling?  
There is sorrow in my heart,  
Sorrow in mine, –  
My beloved is leaving me.**

2. If I were  
A gray cuckoo,  
I would have wings,  
I would fly  
To the edge of the world,  
To search for my beloved.

3. The night flew,  
The second night flew,  
But I see no one,  
Oh, I will sit, sit  
In the cherry orchard  
And bitterly cry to myself.

4. Oh, the old mother  
Went out of the house  
And began to ask her son:  
– Oh son, son,  
What is that bird  
Singing in our grove?

5. – Oh, that is not a bird,  
That is not a bird,  
Singing in our grove,  
Oh, that is a girl  
Betrothed,  
Searching for her fate.

1. If it were not winter  
And not frost in winter,  
I would run  
Barefoot to the birch tree.  
2. I would run  
Barefoot to the birch tree,  
Oh, whether the dew  
Is not shaken from the sage?  
3. Oh, whether it is shaken  
From sage and rue,  
Oh, whether my beloved  
Is here or not?  
4. The girl lies  
In her father’s storeroom,  
The orphan Cossack –  
In the green oak grove.  
5. Her mother  
Carries honey and wine,  
The orphan Cossack  
Asks for winter water.  
6. – My mother,  
How dear you are to me,  
Bring the Cossack  
This little mug of beer.  
7. – My daughter,  
I have not brewed beer,  
I have not carried  
Beer to the Cossacks either.  
8. – Oh, all the bells  
Have rung in the grove,  
Listen, mother,  
Is it for the Cossack?  
9. If it is for service,  
Then lift me up,  
If it is about the Cossack,  
Then advise me.  
10. Chop down, mother,  
A tall sycamore,  
Make for me  
A deep oak coffin.  
11. Bury us  
Together, head to head,  
So that there will be no  
Separation between us.

1. If it were not for winter  
And not for the winter dew,  
I would run  
Barefoot to the birch tree.  
2. I would run  
Barefoot to the birch tree,  
Oh, was the dew  
Not shaken off the sage?  
3. Oh, was it not shaken off  
From the sage and the rue,  
Oh, is my beloved  
Not here?  
4. The girl lies  
In her father’s storeroom,  
The orphan Cossack –  
In the green oak grove.  
5. Her mother  
Carries honey and wine,  
The orphan Cossack  
Asks for winter water.  
6. – My mother,  
How dear you are to me,  
Bring the Cossack  
This mug of beer.  
7. – My daughter,  
I haven’t even brewed beer,  
I have never brought  
Beer to the Cossacks.  
8. – Oh, all the bells  
Rang in the grove,  
Listen, mother,  
Is it for the Cossacks?  
9. If it’s for service,  
Then wake me up,  
If it’s for the Cossacks,  
Then prepare me.  
10. Cut, mother,  
The tall maple,  
Make for me  
A deep oak coffin.  
11. Bury us  
Together head to head,  
So that there is no  
Separation between us.

1. Oh, there on the mountain,  
Oh, there on the steep one,  
A pair of doves  
Were courting,  
With gray wings  
They were embracing.  
2. Oh, somewhere appeared  
A young shooter,  
He killed the male dove,  
Left the female dove.  
3. The female dove neither eats,  
The female dove neither drinks,  
To the steep mountain  
She goes to cry.  
4. – Come here, little dove,  
Seven hundred doves,  
Choose, little dove,  
Which one is yours.  
5. – Oh, give me  
Seven hundred and four,  
There is none  
Like my beloved was.  
6. When she laughs (begins to speak),  
The garden will bloom,  
When she chirps,  
The garden will flower.

1. Oh, on the raft,  
On the little raft,  
On the water,*  
There floats  
A handsome young man  
In a boat,*  
2. And a girl  
Takes water  
With a small bucket,  
Calls  
The Cossack boy  
A falcon:  
3. – Oh, Cossack,  
Little Cossack,  
Falcon,  
Take me  
In your little boat  
With you.  
4. – Oh, I would gladly,  
Little girl,  
Take you,  
But who then  
Will drive  
The little swans?  
5. Somewhere appeared  
A handsome young man,  
Also a shooter,  
He killed, he killed  
The finest  
Swan.  
6. White feathers  
Scattered  
Across the bushes,  
Red blood  
Spilled  
Across the rivers.  
7. Oh, that’s not blood,  
That’s a maiden’s  
Beauty.  
Why is the girl’s  
Hair uncombed?  
8. – For three weeks  
I did not lie in sorrow,**  
On the fourth  
I began  
To get better.  
9. And on the fifth  
I went to the cherry orchard,  
And on the sixth  
I walked  
All the paths.  
10. – And who made these  
Paths?  
– A handsome Cossack  
Went to the girl.  
And he brought her  
Silver and gold,  
11. But she  
Did not take his silver and gold,  
She called him  
A fool:  
12. – Go, go,  
Fool,  
Away from me,  
I have  
A better Cossack  
Than you.

1. Oh, on the raft,  
On the little raft,  
On the water,  
There floats  
A handsome young man  
On a boat.  

2. And the girl  
Takes water  
With a little bucket,  
Calls  
The Cossack young man  
A falcon:  

3. – Oh Cossack,  
Young Cossack,  
Falcon,  
Take me  
On your little boat  
With you.  

4. – Oh, I would be glad,  
Little girl,  
To take you,  
But who then  
Will drive  
The swanlings?  

5. Somewhere appeared  
A handsome young man,  
Also a shooter,  
Killed, killed  
The best  
Swan.  

6. White feathers  
Scattered  
Among the bushes,  
Red blood  
Spread  
Along the rivers.  

7. Oh, that’s not blood,  
That is maiden’s  
Beauty.  
Why does the girl’s  
Hair remain  
Uncombed?  

8. – For three weeks  
I was not in sorrow,  
Lying down,  
On the fourth  
I started  
To recover.  

9. And on the fifth  
Went to the little cherry orchard,  
And on the sixth  
Walked all the little paths.  

10. – And who made  
These little paths?  
– A handsome Cossack  
Walked to the girl.  
And he carried for her  
Silver and gold,  

11. She did not accept  
His silver and gold,  
She called him  
A fool:  

12. – Go, go,  
Fool,  
Away from me,  
I have  
A better Cossack  
Than you.

1. Oh, on the raft,  
On the little raft,  
On the water,*  
There floats  
A handsome young man  
On a boat,*  
2. And the girl  
Takes water  
With a little bucket,  
Calling  
The young Cossack  
A falcon:  
3. – Oh Cossack,  
Young Cossack,  
Falcon,  
Take me  
On your little boat  
With you.  
4. – Oh, I would be glad,  
Girl,  
To take you,  
But who then  
Will herd  
The swan chicks?  
5. Suddenly appeared  
A handsome young man,  
Also a marksman,  
Killed, killed  
The finest  
Swan.  
6. White feathers scattered  
Among the bushes,  
Red blood spilled  
Over the rivers.  
7. Oh, that’s not blood,  
That’s  
A girl’s  
Beauty.  
Why is the girl’s  
Braid  
Uncombed?  
8. – For three weeks  
I lay not in sorrow,**  
On the fourth  
I began  
To recover.  
9. And on the fifth  
I went to the cherry orchard,  
And on the sixth  
I walked  
All the paths.  
10. – But who  
Made these paths?  
– A handsome Cossack  
Went to the girl.  
And he brought her  
Silver and gold,  
11. But she  
Did not take his  
Silver and gold,  
She called him  
A fool:  
12. – Go, go,  
Fool,  
Away from me,  
I have  
A better Cossack  
Than you.

1. Oh, if only I knew the way to Kyiv,  
I would send my darling a gift.  

2. That gift is small,  
Just four little nuts,  
If there’s no way to come,  
Come on foot, my dear.  

3. A little Cossack was riding  
To visit a girl,  
His black horse fell  
On the wide bridge.  

4. They are pulling, pulling Vasylyechko,  
Water is pouring from him,  
Beside him, the girl,  
Beats her heart like a dove.  

5. Oh, if only I knew the way to Kyiv,  
I would send my darling a gift.

1. Oh, if only I knew the way  
To Kyiv,  
I would send my dear one  
A little gift.  
2. That little gift  
Is four nuts,  
If you have no way to come,  
Come, my heart, on foot.  
3. The Cossack was riding, riding  
To visit the girl,  
The black horse fell through  
On the wide bridge.  
4. They pull and pull at Vasylechko,  
Water is flowing from him,  
Next to him, the girl  
Beats her wings like a dove.

1. – Won't you, young girl,

They held hands,

11. – Don’t cry, father, don’t cry, mother,
I am not to blame!
The time has come to go to the army,
It must be done.

Has been dressed,

10. Laid on a wooden bench,

15. And tied his horse  
To the viburnum,

1. A small river flows,

A young recruit  
Asks the colonel:  

2. – Oh, sir colonel,

4. – I drank water, drank cold water,

The dark-haired girl  
Appears to me in my dreams.  

5. Give the horse oats and hay,

Lead the horse behind me.  

6. They carry the body, lead the horse,

The girl stands on the threshold,  
Breaking her white hands.  

7. She breaks her hands, breaks her white little hands,

– I will leave this world, but I won’t find  
A better handsome man!

1. Oh, the Cossack dreamed a dream,

3. "Gypsy girl with rosy cheeks,

7. As the Cossack rode

9. "Get up, get up, Marusya,

Take the Cossack’s son,  
Carry him in your arms."

1. And in the field, hemp

Or won’t my mother allow me?

12. – Do not worry, mother,  
For my Marko,

13. Marko does not worry,

A high mound!

1. Oh, the blue sea has blossomed

2. With various flowers...

3. Oh, with muskets...

And the young girl  
Listened to them,  
10. And got on the ship,  
Went with them.  
11. Went with them...

12. Dozed off, fell asleep

What have you done,  
14. That you have separated me, a young girl,

16. It was fitting for you, young girl,  
To part with your family:  
17. – So farewell, father,

18. Because you will not give me away,

1. On the mountain, the mountain

2. The elder sister

3. She met  
Three bachelors,  
4. They gave Hannusia  
A horse to hold,

6. The sister let

She remembered:

Not to be cut down,

Not to be broken.

1. Oh burn, burn  
Three brothers' scythes.*  
2. Sister Hanunya  
Carried breakfast.  
3. She met  
Three bachelors.  
4. They gave Hanunya  
A horse to hold.  
5. And they themselves sat down  
And ate breakfast.  
6. Hanunya saw  
That it was not true.  
7. She let the horse  
Into the open field.  
8. She herself jumped  
Into the blue sea.  
9. Oh, as she was jumping  
She said:  
10. So that from this sea  
No water could be taken.  
11. In the forest a birch tree,  
So that it would not be cut down.  
12. And in the meadow grass,  
So that it would not be mowed.  
13. From the grass dew,  
So that it would not be collected.  
14. That is not a birch tree,  
That is Hanunya herself.  
15. Oh, that is not grass,  
That is her scythe.  
16. Oh, that is not dew,  
That is her beauty.

Hanunya’s sorrow.

That you slept with the Cossack.

She drank beer,

Her mother found out.  
9. – May a bad hour wash you!  
My whole family  
Has disowned me!

Her mother disowned her,  
And even that Cossack fellow,  
Who thought to play around.

2. – Oh, I would gladly blow,  
The ravine is very deep.  
Oh, I would gladly come,  
The land is very far.  
3. When I was sending my dear  
Off on a journey,  
I gave him a little cuckoo,  
To call early.  
4. I gave him a little cuckoo,  
To call early,  
I took a little nightingale for myself,  
To live joyfully.  
5. The jackdaw flies over the mountains,  
Flying like a rook,  
Pass on to my dear,  
Let her not cry.  
6. Let her not cry,  
I am not grieving here,  
Let her go and marry,  
I will marry here too.  
7. – Oh, may that evil hour  
Marry you,  
And may a little child  
Bind your white hands.

1. In the wide valley  
Vasyl is mowing hay,  
A young girl  
Is carrying a baby to nurse.*  
2. – Young girl,  
I love you,  
When there will be a son,  
I will acknowledge him.  
3. – My mother, my mother,  
There is news:  
I found a small child  
In the hayfields.  
4. – That’s alright, son,  
Bring her into the house,  
We will find  
A mother for this child.  
5. – My mother, my mother,  
Here is more news:  
In the hallway behind the door,  
There stands a girl.  
6. – That’s good, son,  
Lead her into the house, –  
There will be for this child  
A very dear mother.

1. Oh, there Roman is mowing hay,  
And I am a girl carrying lunch.  
2. Oh, she carried it, but did not bring it,  
She carried it, but did not bring it,  
She rested by the Danube.  
3. She rested by the Danube,  
And spoke with the Danube:  
4. Oh Danube, little Danube,  
Advise my little naked one,  
5. Oh, should I drown him,  
Or should I raise my son?  
6. Oh, to drown him would be a sin,  
To raise him would be a shame.  
7. But I endured the shame,  
And I raised my son.  
8. I hung the cradle  
In the forest on an oak tree.  
9. The wind will blow,  
It will rock the son.  
10. The rain will drip,  
It will entertain the son (drizzle over him).  
11. I walked a mile, walked another,  
The child cries, and I hear him.  
12. I must turn back,  
I must earn bread.  
13. I must earn bread,  
I must feed the child.

1. – Oh, you, blacksmith,  
Blacksmith Mark,  
Why don’t you forge  
From evening till morning?  

2. Do you, blacksmith,  
Run out of iron?  
Or do you, blacksmith,  
Lack steel?  

3. – Hey, oh yes, I have  
Steel upon steel,  
Hey, oh yes, I have  
Sorrow upon sorrow.  

4. Hey, oh yes, I have  
A girl named Kateryna,  
Hey, and she has  
Brought me shame.  

5. Hey, she has  
Brought me shame,  
Because last night in the evening  
She gave birth to a child.  

6. Because last night in the evening  
She gave birth to a child,  
Hey, and at midnight  
She drowned it in the churn.  

7. Hey, and at midnight  
She drowned it in the churn,  
Hey, at dawn  
She prayed to God:  

8. Hey, give me, God,  
Snow and frost,  
Hey, that the wind may blow  
On paths and roads.  

9. Hey, so that no one knows  
Where I have been walking,  
Hey, so that no one knows  
That I drowned the child.

1. – Oh, blacksmith,  
Oh, blacksmith Mark,  
Why don’t you forge  
From evening till morning?  
Hey, hilya, hilya.  

2. Don’t you have  
Any iron?  
Don’t you have  
Enough steel?  
Hey, hilya, hilya.  

3. – I have  
Iron and steel,  
I have  
A girl named Kateryna.  
Hey, hilya, hilya.  

4. I have  
A girl named Kateryna,  
She brought me  
Fame and honor.  
Hey, hilya, hilya.  

5. She brought me  
Fame and honor,  
And in the evening  
She made the bed.  
Hey, hilya, hilya.  

6. And in the evening  
She made the bed,  
And at midnight  
She gave birth to a son.  
Hey, hilya, hilya.  

7. And at midnight  
She gave birth to a son,  
At dawn  
She drowned him in the well.  
Hey, hilya, hilya.

1. On Holy Thursday evening  
The Lord came for a conversation.  
2. He met a girl with water,  
With cold spring water.  
3. – Oh, girl, give me water to drink,  
To sprinkle sinful souls.  
4. Your water is very pure,  
Gathered from the sea and rivers.  
5. Gathered from the sea and rivers,  
Dripped from a maple leaf.  
6. Oh, this water is very pure,  
But the girl herself is impure.  
7. Oh, girl, you had seven sons,  
You buried them in this water.  
8. The girl stood, frightened,  
Water spilled from the buckets.  
9. – Oh girl, do not be afraid,  
Go home and say goodbye.  
10. Go home and say goodbye,  
Go to church and confess.  
11. When the girl entered the church,  
She sank into the ground up to her knees.

1. Under the grove,  
With a beaten hat,  
That way rode  
The major's son.  
2. He met a girl—  
Seven years old:  
– Hello, girl—  
Seven years old!  
3. Hello, girl—  
Seven years old,  
Guess these riddles for me;  
4. If you guess—  
You will be mine,  
If you don’t guess—  
You will be a fool.  
5. What runs  
Without a herdsman?  
What shines  
Without a pattern?  
6. What twines  
Around a little tree?  
What grows  
Without roots?  
7. What cries  
Without tears?  
What plays  
Without strings?  
8. What burns  
Without embers?  
Who lives  
Without a wife?  
9. – You are a fool,  
Major's son,  
Even though you have  
A high rank!  
10. Water runs  
Without a herdsman,  
The moon shines  
Without a pattern.  
11. A vine twines  
Around a little tree,  
A stone grows  
Without roots.  
12. The violin cries—  
Without tears,  
The flute plays—  
Without strings.  
13. The sun burns  
Without embers,  
The Cossack lives  
Without a wife.

1. Let me, mother,  
Dig a well,  
Hey, will the girls  
Come to draw water from it?*  
2. Oh, the girls came  
To draw water from it,  
Hey, but my girls  
The mother does not let go.  
3. Let me, mother,  
Build a church,  
Hey, will the girls  
Come to praise God?  
4. Oh, the girls came  
To praise God,  
Hey, but my girls  
The mother does not let go.  
5. Let me, mother,  
Lie on the bench,  
Hey, will the girls  
Come to see the dead?  
6. Oh, the girls came  
To see the dead,  
Hey, but my girls  
The mother does not let go.  
7. – Mother, my mother,  
How unfaithful you are,  
Hey, the illnesses are already  
Standing in his yard!  
8. Oh, the girl came  
To see the dead,  
Hey, prayed to God,  
Wanted to kiss.  
9. And that dead man jumped up,  
Clung to the neck:  
– Hey, give me the coat, mother,  
Let’s go to the wedding!

1. Let me, mother,  
Dig the well,  
Will the girls come  
To fetch water?  
2. The girls came  
To fetch water,  
But my beloved’s  
Mother does not let her.  
3. Let me, mother,  
Die on Sunday,  
Will the girls come  
To lay me out?  
4. The girls came  
To lay me out,  
But my beloved’s  
Mother does not let her.  
5. – Let me go, mother,  
For a while, for an hour,  
Let me look  
At that coffin.  
6. The girl came,  
Standing at the threshold,  
They poured out the girl  
And small (tiny) tears:  
7. – These are the feet  
That walked to me,  
These are the hands  
That brought gifts.  
8. These [lips]  
Kissed me,  
These lips  
Spoke to me.  
9. Here the dead man woke up,  
Seized by the neck:  
– Come on, mother, get the coat,  
Let’s go to the wedding!  
10. This fame  
Spread throughout the world,  
That the dead man and the living  
Became one at the ritual cloth.

1. When I was little,  
In my mother’s (arms),  
A gypsy woman told my fortune,  
Took me by the hand.*  
2. The gypsy woman told my fortune,  
Took me by the hand:  
“Don’t marry, girl,  
You are still young.”  
5. My father is rich,  
He built a bridge,  
Wide and long,  
A thousand versts.  
6. An iron road,  
There goes a train,  
In the front carriage  
It carries a girl.  
7. The girl was sitting,  
Looking out the window,  
Leaned over and fell  
To the bottom of the sea.  
3. Don’t marry, girl,  
You will know sorrow,  
In the blue sea  
You will drown.  
4. “Don’t believe, my dear,  
Don’t believe anyone,  
Believe, my dear,  
Only me.”

1. The sun has set beyond the window,  
Beyond the cherry orchard,  
Let us love each other, let us be in love,  
But who rejoices in this?  
2. The girl took the young Cossack  
And led him to the orchard,  
She spread a white bed,  
Laid him down to sleep.  
3. She herself sat by the window,  
Fell into sorrow,  
That she had fallen in love  
So young.  
4. – Do not grieve, dear girl,  
You will be mine,  
I have a barrel of honey,  
And another of wine.  
5. I have a barrel of honey,  
And another of wine,  
I have a little carriage  
Gilded with gold.  
6. I have a little carriage  
And also a black horse,  
So we will sit and ride  
To my mother-in-law’s.  
7. – Oh, here for you, my mother-in-law,  
Is this barrel of wine,  
Give me your daughter  
At least for a day or two.  
8. – Oh, was I ever foolish  
Or out of my mind,  
To give you my daughter  
Even for a day?  
9. Her face is like an apple,  
Like a rose blossom,  
And whoever takes her,  
It is for a lifetime.

1. The sun set behind the window,  
Behind the garden-vineyard,  
They loved each other, they made love,  
But who is happy about it?  

2. They loved each other, they made love,  
But who is happy about it?  
She took the young man by the hand  
And led him to the garden.  

3. She took the young man by the hand,  
Became sorrowful,  
In the cherry orchard  
She lay down to sleep.  

4. – Don’t be sad, young girl,  
You will be mine,  
I have a barrel of beer,  
And another of wine.  

5. I have a barrel of beer,  
And another of wine,  
I have a gilded carriage.  

6. I have a gilded carriage,  
And also a black horse,  
And I will sit and ride  
To my dear mother-in-law.  

7. When he arrived at the mother-in-law’s,  
He said: – Good day!  
Sell me daughter Yavdoshka  
Even for just one day.  

8. – I have a daughter Yavdoshka  
Like a rose blossom,  
And whoever loves her faithfully,  
Will take her forever.

1. The sun set behind the window,  
Behind the vineyard garden,  
They loved each other, they made love,  
But who was happy about it?  

2. They loved each other, they made love,  
But who was happy about it?  
She took the young man by the hand  
And led him into the garden.  

3. She took the young man by the hand,  
Became sorrowful,  
In the cherry orchard  
She lay down to sleep.  

4. – Don’t be sad, girl,  
You will be mine,  
I have a barrel of beer,  
And another of wine.  

5. I have a barrel of beer,  
And another of wine,  
I have a gilded little carriage.  

6. I have a little carriage  
And also a black horse,  
And I will sit down and ride  
To my mother-in-law gladly.  

7. When he arrived at his mother-in-law’s,  
He said: – Good day!  
Sell me your daughter Yavdoshka  
Even for one day.  

8. – My daughter Yavdoshka  
Is like a pink blossom,  
And whoever truly loves her  
Will take her forever.

1. Hey, if only God would grant
Good weather in the evening,
Hey, I would take buckets
And go for water.
2. Hey, but the girl
Had not yet drawn water,
And she already called
The young Cossack a falcon:
3. – Hey, oh Cossack,
Cossack-falcon,
Hey, take me
With you in the boat.
4. Hey, but the girl
Had not yet sat in the boat,
Hey, when a huge wave
Suddenly broke loose.
5. Hey, when the huge wave
Suddenly broke loose,
It washed the young girl
Out of the boat.
6. Hey, three Cossacks
Came from the field,
They started to give water
To a black horse.
7. Hey, one says:
– The pike-fish is playing.
Hey, and another says:
– The girl is drowning.
8. Hey, and the third says:
– Whether it happens or not,
I will save
The young girl.
9. – Hey, save me,
You, my dear heart,
Hey, and you will receive
A reward from my father.
10. Hey, and you will receive
A reward from my father,
Hey, and from my mother
Great thanks.
11. Hey, from my father
A black horse,
And from my mother
A gold ruble.
12. – Hey, I don’t want
To take payment from you,
I only want to take you
As my wife.
13. – Hey, I’d rather
Drown in this sea,
Hey, than be
Your wife.
14. Hey, I’d rather
Eat yellow sands,
Hey, than sit down
To dinner with you.
15. – Hey, then bring
The thickest pole,
To push even further
The young girl.
16. Hey, the girl Natalka
Drowned,
Hey, only the red kerchief
Floated to the surface.

1. Oh God, grant  
Good weather from the evening,  
I will take buckets  
And go for water.*  
2. So I took the buckets  
And went for water,  
There I met  
A handsome Cossack.  
3. – Oh you, Cossack,  
Gray falcon,  
Take me  
In your boat with you.  
4. As soon as the girl  
Stepped into the boat,  
The swift water  
Flooded the boat.  
5. And there the boys  
Were watering the horses,  
They saw the girl  
On the sea.  
6. – Oh come here, boys,  
A long pole,  
I will rescue  
The young girl.  
7. – Oh rescue me, rescue me,  
My little Cossack,  
You will receive  
A gift for me:  
8. Oh from my father,  
A black horse,  
And from my mother,  
A saddle for it.  
9. Oh from my mother,  
A saddle for it,  
From myself  
A golden ring.  
10. – But I don’t want  
To take these gifts,  
I only want  
To take you as my wife.  
11. – Oh, it’s better for me  
To eat bitter wormwood,  
Than to sit with you  
On the estate.  
12. Oh, it’s better for me  
To drown in the sea,  
Than to stand with you  
At the wedding.  
13. – Oh give me, boys,  
A long pole,  
Let me drown  
The young girl.  
14. Oh [the Cossack took]  
A long pole,  
The Cossack drowned  
The young girl.  
15. Oh the girl Hulianka  
Drowned,  
Only the red kerchief  
Floated up.

1. Die, old man, die,  
I will follow the young one,  
I do not seek happiness –  
I will find a better fate.  

2. The old man died, died,  
I followed the young one,  
I lost happiness,  
I found a worse fate.  

3. In the field a grave  
Spoke with the wind,  
Oh, there the young woman  
Woke the old man:  

4. – Get up, old man, get up,  
Rise, my lord,  
Look, my lord,  
At your fields.  

5. Oh, two fields of rye  
To feed the children,  
And the third of wheat  
To sow in the spring.  

6. Oh, I cooked fish,  
I did not grieve,  
It was good to live with the old man –  
But I did not appreciate it.  

7. – At first you said:  
– The old man with a beard.  
But now you say:  
– It was good with you.

1. Oh, there is a little lake in the field,  
There floated a little bucket,  
Pine wood staves, maple bottom,  
Let us not shun each other, my heart!  

2. If you shun me,  
People will laugh,  
Let’s join the right hands,  
And go to church to get married.  

3. Oh, we came to the church,  
But did not find the priest at home.  
Oh, is it yours, or is it mine  
Unhappy fate?  

4. – Oh, I would be glad to marry,  
To speak with you, my heart,  
The unloved one lies on my right hand,  
And I am afraid to wake him.  

5. Oh, there is a little lake in the field,  
There floated a little bucket,  
Pine wood staves, maple bottom,  
Let us not shun each other, my heart!  

6. There floated another one, too,  
Three days with water.  
– Come out, girl, come out, little fish,  
I want to talk with you!  

7. How can I come to you  
And talk with you?  
Oh, the unloved one lies on my right hand,  
And I am afraid to wake him.  

8. – Oh, my dear girl,  
Step away from the unloved one!  
I will kill the unloved one with a bow, like that dove,  
You, my dear girl!  

9. – Oh, whether you hit or miss,  
You will only separate me from him;  
Oh, whether you strike or not,  
You will only lose my life!  

10. You, young Cossack,  
You have a black horse,  
Saddle the horse, ride out of the yard,  
Because you are not mine, and I am not yours.  

11. The Cossack saddles the horse  
And speaks to the horse:  
– Trot, my horse, trot, black one,  
All the way to gentle Danube!  

12. When he arrives at the Danube,  
He stands, looks, and thinks:  
– Should I drown myself, or fall off the horse,  
Or return back?  

13. If I fall off the horse – I will be hurt,  
If I fall into the sea – I won’t live,  
If I return home,  
I will live – I will get married.

1. Oh, there is a small lake in the field,  
A little bucket was floating there,  
Pine bark, and an oak bottom,  
Do not be ashamed, my heart.  

2. – Oh, my dear girl,  
Turn away from the unloved one,  
Come out, girl, come out, fish,  
I want to talk to you.  

3. – Oh, I would be glad to come out,  
But I don't know what to do, –  
The unloved one lies on my right arm,  
And I am afraid to wake him.  

4. – Help me, God,  
To take this girl,  
I will shoot, I will aim  
At the green oak.

1. Oh, there is a little lake in the field,  
There floated a little bucket,  
Pine hoops, and an oak bottom,  
Do not shun me, my heart.*  
2. – Oh, my dear girl,  
Turn away from the unloved one,  
Come out, girl, come out, little fish,  
I will talk to you.  
3. – Oh, I would be glad to come out,  
But I do not know what to do, –  
The unloved one lies on the right arm,  
And I am afraid to wake him.  
4. – Help me, God,  
To take this girl,  
I will shoot, I will aim  
At the green oak.

Filename: Vinnytsia_ballads_620_Oj ty sad, ty mij sad
translation:
1. – Oh, you garden, my garden,  
You are flourishing,  
Oh, where are you going, my friend,  
Setting off?  

2. – Oh, I will go  
To the China-town,  
And to China-town  
To the fair.  

3. Oh, I will buy a ship  
For one hundred to two hundred rubles,  
For one hundred to two hundred rubles  
And a half.  

4. For one hundred to two hundred rubles  
And a half,  
I will settle my wife  
With the child.  

5. The ship sails,  
While the Danube roars,  
And my beloved sits,  
Burning like a candle.  

6. And my beloved sits,  
Burning like a candle,  
And the child cries,  
Like a crow caws.  

7. And the child cries,  
Like a crow caws,  
And my dear walks  
Along the shore.  

8. And my dear walks  
Along the shore:  
– Oh give me, my dear,  
At least your right hand!  

9. Oh give me, my dear,  
At least your right hand,  
I will kiss you  
And also the child.  

10. – Oh, I will not return  
Nor look back,  
Oh, you beat me  
Even with a whip.  

11. Oh, you beat me  
Even with a whip,  
You never called me  
Mistress.  

12. Oh, you beat me  
Even with a rod,  
You never called me  
Lady of the house.

Filename: Vinnytsia_ballads_621_Oj ty sad, ty moj sad
translation:
1. – Oh, you garden, my garden,  
The green garden,  
Not in time do you bloom,  
You are blossoming.*  
2. Not in time do you bloom,  
You are blossoming,  
Oh where, my friend,  
Are you going?  
3. – I am going  
To Kopaigorodok,  
To Kopaigorodok  
And to the fair.  
4. To Kopaigorodok  
And to the fair,  
I will buy a ship  
For one hundred twenty rubles.  
5. For one hundred twenty rubles  
And a half,  
I will plant a wife  
And a child as well.  
6. I will plant a wife  
And a child as well  
And send them on the Danube  
Through the middle.  
7. And the ship sails,  
The Danube roars,  
And the child cries,  
My heart aches.

1. Oh, he planned, he wished,  
He did not take a meek woman.  
2. He did not take a meek woman,  
If only God would accept her!  
3. The dear one came at night,  
Caught his beloved on the stove.  
4. Covered her with a cloth  
And also pressed down with his fist.  
5. And also pressed down with his fist...  
He took his hat and left.  
6. He took his hat and left,  
Out to the street to the girls.  
7. The girls are sitting in a row,  
Singing songs together.  
8. One girl was different,  
She cried and left.  
9. She cried and left...  
Probably the beloved’s sister.  
10. She runs home  
And tells no one.  
11. – Oh, our father, our father,  
There is our son-in-law on the street!  
12. – Let him, daughter, have his fun,  
The Lord will punish him!  
13. For a small roof,  
For someone else's child.

1. The gray nightingale,  
Gray... the gray nightingale,  
You have... you have a thin voice.  

2. Chirp for me,  
Chirp... chirp for me,  
Because I... because I am in a foreign land.  

3. Because I am in a foreign land,  
Because I... because I am in a foreign land,  
There is no... no kin near me.  

4. There is no kin or family,  
There is no... no kin or family,  
No faithful... no faithful wife.  

5. I did not marry a loving woman,  
Did not... did not marry a loving woman,  
So I... so I will not live with her.  

6. I will go serve in the army,  
I will go... I will go serve in the army,  
So that I... so that I do not live with my beloved.  

7. I came from the army in the morning,  
I came... I came from the army in the morning,  
And found... found my beloved on the bench.

1. Oh, I will go up the hill,  
I will stand and look into the valley.  

2. I will stand and look into the valley  
At the red viburnum.  

3. On the red viburnum  
Is a little nightingale.  

4. The little nightingale  
Has a thin voice.  

5. Don’t chirp until dawn,  
Don’t cause me sorrow.  

6. Don’t cause me sorrow,  
Because I am in a foreign land.  

7. Because I am in a foreign land,  
I have no kin near me.  

8. No kin, no family,  
No faithful wife.  

9. I didn’t marry a sensible woman,  
And I will not live with her.  

10. I will go serve in the army,  
So I won’t live with her.  

11. I have not yet served in the army,  
But I already married a wife.  

12. I covered her hands with cloth,  
Then with my fist on top.  

13. I grabbed my hat and went  
To the street to the girls.  

14. – Good evening to you, girls,  
I am left without a wife.  

15. All the girls said:  
– Hello, hello, Ivan!  

16. Only one was silent,  
Turned around and walked away.  

17. She cried and walked away,  
Because she was the woman’s sister.  

18. – Good evening, our father,  
Your son-in-law is already out having fun.  

19. Good evening, dear father,  
The son-in-law is at the gathering.  

20. – Let him have fun, daughter,  
God will punish him  

21. And also his family  
For our child.

1. Oh, I will go out on the hill,  
I will stand and look into the valley.  
2. I will stand and look into the valley  
At the red viburnum.  
3. On the red viburnum  
Is a little nightingale.  
4. The little nightingale  
Has a thin voice.  
5. Don’t chirp until dawn,  
Don’t cause me sorrow.  
6. Don’t cause me sorrow,  
Because I am in a foreign land.  
7. Because I am in a foreign land,  
I have no kin with me.  
8. No family,  
No faithful wife.  
9. I did not marry a wise woman,  
And I will not live with her.  
10. I will go to serve in the army,  
So that I don’t have to live with her.  
11. I have not yet served in the army,  
But I already married a wife.  
12. He covered her hands with cloth,  
And on top with his fist.  
13. He took his hat and went  
Out to the street to the girls.  
14. – Good evening to you, girls,  
I am left without a wife.  
15. All the girls said:  
– Hello, hello, Ivan!  
16. Only one remained silent,  
Turned around and left.  
17. She cried and left,  
Because she was the woman’s sister.  
18. – Good evening, our father,  
Your son-in-law is already having fun.  
19. Good evening, dear father,  
The son-in-law is at the party.  
20. – Let him have fun, daughter,  
God will punish him  
21. And also his family  
For our child.

1. A mother had one daughter  
And she bathed her in a little wreath.  
2. She bathed her, swaddled her,  
But did not foresee her happiness and fate.  
3. Did not foresee her happiness and fate,  
She gave her in marriage to a drunkard.  
4. And the drunkard drinks, carouses,  
Goes home, scolds his wife:  
5. – Run, wife, drown yourself,  
Because you are no good to me.  
6. The wife came to the Danube,  
And spoke to the Danube:  
7. – Oh Danube, little Danube,  
Take me in for the winter.  
8. You accepted father, you accepted mother,  
Accept me, a young one.  
9. I have four children,  
And I am expecting a fifth.  
10. You, yellow sand,  
Feed my little children.

Filename: Vinnytsia_ballads_629_Oj pidu ja v lis po drova
translation:
1. Oh, I will go to the forest for firewood
And break some branches,
A foolish mind misled me
And I went... I went in the wrong direction.*
2. I am in a foreign land
Without father or mother,
Only in the cherry orchard
Nightingales are singing... singing.
3. Oh sing, nightingales,
With two voices,
And I will go to the cherry orchard,
I will drown... drown in tears.
4. Oh, I will tear a flower from a rose
And set it afloat on the water:
– Float, float, rose flower,
All the way... all the way to my family.
5. The flower floated, the flower floated,
And stopped by the shore,
The mother came to fetch water,
And recognized the flower.
6. – My rosy flower,
Why have you withered so?
My daughter, my only one,
Why... why have you grown old?
7. Did you marry off your son,
Or give your daughter away?
– A cruel fate befell me,
That's why... that's why I have grown old.

Filename: Vinnytsia_ballads_629v_Oj pidu ja v lis po drova
translation:
1. Oh, I will go to the forest for firewood  
And break off some branches,  
A foolish mind has led me  
To a strange... to a strange place.  

2. I am in a strange land  
With neither father nor mother,  
Only in the cherry orchard  
The nightingales sing... sing.  

3. Oh sing, nightingales,  
With two voices,  
And I will go to the cherry orchard,  
Drench... drench myself with tears.  

4. Oh, I will tear a flower from the rose  
And set it afloat on the water:  
– Float, float, rose flower,  
Right to... right to my kin.  

5. The flower floated, the flower floated,  
And stopped on the shore,  
The mother came to fetch water,  
And recognized the flower.  

6. – My rose flower,  
Why have you withered so?  
My daughter, my only one,  
Why... why have you grown old?  

7. Did you marry off your son,  
Or give away your daughter?  
– I was caught by a cruel fate,  
That is... that is why I have grown old.

translation:

2. I am in a foreign land  
Without father or mother,

Nightingales are singing... singing.

3. Oh, sing, nightingales,  
With two voices,  
And I will go to the cherry orchard,  
And drown... drown in tears.

4. Oh, I will pluck a flower from the rosebush  
And set it afloat on the water:

(all the way) to... to my kin.

Why... why have you grown old?
